## Customer Idea
Eco-Float Voltaic Platform (EFVP) for eco-adaptive floating photovoltaic systems. The disclosed concept concerns a floating solar photovoltaic system using a rectangular float with a divided top platform and four walls, a cross-shaped support fitted between the walls, four pontoons each retained in openings defined by the cross-shape, bifacial solar PV mounting on two triangular supports, optional concrete-block anchoring with plastic-coated stainless steel wires, and optional sensing/data logging for temperature, irradiance, current, and voltage with wireless transmission to a central database.

## Clarifications
User instructed not to ask clarifying questions during scoping and to use reasonable defaults.
Confirmed defaults:
1. Primary novelty focus: full combined system, with structural floating platform treated as core differentiator.
2. Bifacial PV panel treated as essential.
3. Sensor/telemetry package treated as secondary/add-on subsystem.
4. Divided top platform interpreted as segmented top deck with side mounting regions around a central support zone.
5. Intended deployment assumed to be calm inland waters such as reservoirs/ponds/lakes.

## Confirmed Scope

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

A prior-art search of floating photovoltaic platform references found a very close recent patent family that discloses the same core structural combination as the submitted EFVP concept: a rectangular float with a divided top platform and four walls, a cross-shaped support fitted between those walls, four pontoons retained in openings formed by the cross-shape, and a bifacial photovoltaic panel mounted on opposed triangular braces/supports [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Independent older references also disclose major portions of the concept in split form, including floating PV structures with separate buoyancy bodies and triangular PV support brackets [US11290054B2], bifacial floating PV modules [US11245352B2], framed floating PV stages with central support members and distributed buoyancy elements [US11319035B2], modular floating PV support frames [US9849945B2], and weighted/anchored floating PV mooring arrangements [US10411643B2; US12494737B2; US12391340B2].

**Types of Solutions Identified**
- Integrated floating PV platforms with dedicated float/frame architecture
- Modular buoyancy-body plus panel-support structures
- Bifacial floating PV module systems
- Weighted and anchored mooring systems for floating solar arrays
- Separate support-body / floating-body architectures for bifacial module cooling and rear-side irradiance capture

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Coverage Status | Representative References | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Rectangular float body with divided top platform and four walls | STRONG | US12401313B1, US12407293B1, US11319035B2 (partial), US9849945B2 (partial) | Exact family match found; older partial analogs also present |
| F2 | Cross-shaped support retaining four pontoons in four openings | STRONG | US12401313B1, US12407293B1, US10784814B2 (partial), US9712108B2 (partial) | Exact family match found; older art shows openings/cells/lattice or distributed buoyancy, but less exact |
| F3 | Bifacial PV panel on two triangular supports | STRONG | US12401313B1, US12407293B1, US11290054B2, US11245352B2 | Exact family match plus older split disclosures for triangular support and bifacial floating PV |
| F4 | Concrete-block / weighted mooring with wires/lines | STRONG | US12401313B1, US12407293B1, US10411643B2, US12494737B2, US12391340B2 | Weighted anchoring/mooring well represented |
| F5 | Temperature / irradiance sensing with wireless logger | MODERATE | US12401313B1, US12407293B1 | Exact-family coverage found; weaker independent older evidence in this search set |
| F6 | Current/voltage monitoring via junction box and wireless telemetry | MODERATE | US12401313B1, US12407293B1 | Exact-family coverage found; limited independent older evidence in final screened set |
| F7 | Electrical cable to load | MODERATE | US12407293B1, WO/US-family variants | Standard power-delivery element |

### Technology / Prior-Art Trends

The art is dense around floating photovoltaic supports, modular float assemblies, and mooring systems for inland-water or marine solar deployment. A recurring trend is separation of buoyancy elements from the panel-support frame, rather than a monolithic rectangular float body. Another trend is using support geometries that improve rear-side exposure for bifacial modules [US11245352B2; US10784814B2]. Anchoring with weights, concrete blocks, lines, ropes, or equivalent mooring members is also common [US10411643B2; US12494737B2; US12391340B2].

### Risk Assessment

The novelty-search risk is **high** because the core combination F1-F3 is directly disclosed by recent exact-family references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1], and the secondary features are also disclosed there. Even ignoring that family, older references collectively cover most of the architecture in split form.

- **Time Period:** Older floating PV art plus close newer family references found in current database coverage

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search quality is **good for a first-pass novelty assessment** of the disclosed architecture.
- Full-text review was performed for retained A/B references used in the Feature Matrix.
- Some semantic hits were noisy and required exclusion.
- The current report relies primarily on patent literature and machine-accessible English-language content.
- No legal conclusions are offered here; this is a prior-art coverage assessment only.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis:
- **F1 (rectangular float body with divided top platform and four walls):** clearly disclosed in the recent exact-family references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Older independent analogs also show framed floating bodies and distributed structural frame layouts [US11319035B2; US9849945B2].
- **F2 (cross-shaped support retaining four pontoons in four openings):** clearly disclosed in the recent exact-family references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Older independent references show partial analogs such as internal lattices/cells or support frames with multiple openings/through-holes [US9712108B2; US10784814B2].
- **F3 (bifacial PV panel mounted on two triangular supports):** clearly disclosed in the recent exact-family references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Older independent art separately discloses triangular floating PV support brackets [US11290054B2] and bifacial floating PV module structures [US11245352B2; US10784814B2].
- **F4 (corner mooring with block anchors and wires/lines):** disclosed by the recent exact-family references and supported by older floating-solar mooring art using concrete blocks, weights, ropes, anchors, or connecting lines [US10411643B2; US12494737B2; US12391340B2].
- **F5/F6 (monitoring/telemetry):** disclosed in the recent exact-family references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Independent older support in the screened final set is weaker, but these are secondary features.
- **F7 (power cable to load):** a standard downstream implementation and expressly disclosed in the marine-system family member [US12407293B1].

Because every core feature (F1-F3) has at least one earlier reference in the identified set with Y or Y1 coverage, and because the exact full combination is also directly present in retained references, the search record supports a non-novel coverage outcome for the disclosed concept.

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The core structural architecture is directly disclosed by at least two highly relevant retained references [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. In addition, older independent references provide reinforcing split coverage for triangular PV mounting, bifacial floating modules, internal frame/open-cell concepts, and floating solar anchoring [US11290054B2; US11245352B2; US10784814B2; US10411643B2].

Top anticipating references: US12401313B1, US12407293B1, and US11290054B2.

## Limitations

- Search was limited to machine-accessible patent literature surfaced in the current session.
- Non-patent literature was not retained in the final screened set.
- Some non-English patent hits were semantic false positives and were excluded after full-text check.
- Publication/legal-status details and IPC/CPC fields were only included where available from returned patent-detail payloads.
- The report is a prior-art coverage assessment, not a legal patentability opinion.

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

Rationale: the search identified retained references that disclose the same core combination of rectangular float body, cross-shaped pontoon-retention support, and bifacial PV mounting on triangular supports [US12401313B1; US12407293B1]. Even aside from those exact-family references, older floating-PV references disclose substantial portions of the structure in split form, reducing any uncovered gap in the main architecture **Invention Title**: Upper Inner Lining Manufacturing with One-Piece Heel Lining Integrated to Waterproof Bootie  
**Domain**: Footwear upper construction; inner lining pattern design; waterproof bootie integration for shoes/boots  
**Key Technical Problem**: Conventional upper-inner-lining constructions can leave visible seam splits at the heel center and at the interface between collar-top lining and waterproof bootie, which may reduce comfort, create pressure points, and limit heel-fit definition.  
**Proposed Solution**: Replace the collar-top lining with a fully one-piece heel lining pattern that extends down to and interfaces with the waterproof bootie/insole region, incorporates collar-top foam into the heel lining, removes the conventional collar-top lining / waterproof-bootie seam at about 75% of bootie height, and covers the heel-center zig-zag bootie seam with the new full lining.  
**Key Claims**:  
- A fully one-piece heel lining pattern replacing a separate collar-top lining  
- Extension of the heel lining pattern to the waterproof bootie/insole region  
- Incorporation of collar-top foam into the heel lining construction  
- Elimination of a seam between collar-top lining and waterproof bootie at an upper bootie height region  
- Coverage of the heel-center waterproof-bootie seam by the new lining structure  
- Resulting improvement in visual cleanliness, wearer comfort, and heel-fit pocket definition  

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults".

1. **Is the invention directed primarily to a footwear upper construction for a shoe/boot with a waterproof inner bootie?**  
   Options: (a) yes, waterproof footwear with internal bootie, (b) general non-waterproof footwear lining, (c) both  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) yes, waterproof footwear with internal bootie** — Gore-Tex bootie integration is central in the disclosure

2. **How should “Gore-Tex bootie” be treated for search scope?**  
   Options: (a) as brand-specific Gore-Tex only, (b) as any waterproof/breathable bootie or membrane liner, (c) both with broader emphasis  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) both with broader emphasis** — this best captures brand-specific and generic prior art terminology

3. **What is the intended claim focus for the search?**  
   Options: (a) pattern/layout construction only, (b) construction plus comfort/fit effects, (c) manufacturing method steps only  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) construction plus comfort/fit effects** — the disclosure emphasizes both structural layout and resulting comfort/heel-fit benefits

4. **How should the “75% Gore-Tex height” limitation be interpreted?**  
   Options: (a) as an approximate upper bootie-height seam-removal location, (b) as a strict dimensional limitation, (c) ignore as nonessential  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) as an approximate upper bootie-height seam-removal location** — the text reads as a construction-region indicator rather than a precise measurement

5. **What type of footwear should be assumed for the search baseline?**  
   Options: (a) athletic shoe/sneaker upper, (b) outdoor/hiking shoe or boot, (c) any lasted footwear upper  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) any lasted footwear upper** — broadest reasonable scope for novelty assessment while still covering athletic and outdoor embodiments

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Waterproof bootie central? | (a) yes, waterproof footwear with internal bootie |
| 2 | Gore-Tex treatment? | (c) both, with broader waterproof-bootie emphasis |
| 3 | Search focus? | (b) construction plus comfort/fit effects |
| 4 | Meaning of 75% height? | (a) approximate upper bootie-height seam-removal location |
| 5 | Footwear type baseline? | (c) any lasted footwear upper |

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

A prior art search was conducted for a footwear upper-inner-lining construction centered on a one-piece heel lining replacing a separate collar-top lining, extending to a waterproof bootie/insole region, integrating padding in the heel/collar region, reducing or relocating liner/bootie seams in the upper heel area, and covering internal rear seams for improved comfort and heel fit. The search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the individual core features and several references covering adjacent combinations, particularly in waterproof bootie integration, seam relocation, continuous inner sleeve constructions, and padded heel/collar structures [US12564246B2; US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; CA3077769A1; EP3654796A1]. No single located reference was found to clearly disclose the entire exact claimed combination as described, but the core feature set is substantially covered across multiple earlier references.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Waterproof bootie/sockliner constructions attached to upper or insole [US12564241B2; US12501960B2; EP3654796A1]
- One-piece or shoe-shaped inner sleeve / inner sock constructions [US11058178B2; US12302989B2]
- Heel/collar lining with integrated pad or foam structures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1; US12520908B2; US12484666B2]
- Rear-seam relocation away from heel center to side regions [US20210093045A1; US20040205982A1]
- Seam covering over waterproof inner sleeve or bootie seams [US12302989B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Final Coverage | Representative References | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | One-piece heel lining pattern | STRONG | US11058178B2, US6367166B1, CA3077769A1 | Continuous/unitary inner liner concepts present, though exact collar-top replacement framing varies |
| F2 | Lining extended to bootie/insole | STRONG | US12564241B2, US12501960B2, EP3654796A1, US12564246B2 | Well covered |
| F3 | Collar foam integrated in heel lining | STRONG | US12564246B2, CA3077769A1, US12520908B2, US12484666B2 | Strong direct and analogous support |
| F4 | Removal/relocation of upper bootie-lining seam | STRONG | US20210093045A1, US12564246B2, US20040205982A1, US12564241B2 | Strongly covered via seam relocation and moved attachment zones |
| F5 | Heel-center bootie seam covered by lining | STRONG | US12302989B2, US12520908B2, US20210093045A1 | Best support from seam-cover and seam-avoidance art; exact “new fully lining” wording not found |
| F6 | Comfort and fit improvement | STRONG | US20210093045A1, US12484666B2, US10314362B2 | Result feature, widely present |
| F7 | Manufacturing approach | STRONG | US12501960B2, US10952502B2, US11484091B2 | Well covered |

### Technology / Prior-Art Themes
- Waterproof breathable footwear increasingly uses internal bootie or inner-sleeve architectures rather than simple loose liners [EP3654796A1; US12564241B2].
- Seam management for comfort is a recurring theme, especially moving seams away from rear heel center and covering seam junctions [US20210093045A1; US12302989B2; US20040205982A1].
- Heel/collar fit is often improved by integrated pads, compressible layers, or heel counter-adjacent foam structures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1; US12484666B2].

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk is **high** from a prior-art coverage perspective because every core feature has at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 support, even though the exact full combination was not located in one single reference [US12564246B2; US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; CA3077769A1; EP3654796A1]. This is a prior-art coverage summary only, not a patentability opinion.

## Customer Idea
Upper inner lining manufacturing for footwear using a fully one-piece heel lining pattern that replaces collar top lining, extends to a waterproof breathable bootie/insole region, integrates collar top foam into the heel lining, removes a seam between collar top lining and waterproof bootie at approximately 75% bootie height, and covers a heel-center zig-zag bootie seam.

## Clarifications
Defaults confirmed by user.
- Scope centers on waterproof footwear with internal bootie.
- "Gore-Tex bootie" treated both brand-specifically and generically as waterproof/breathable bootie or membrane liner.
- Search focus includes construction plus comfort/fit effects.
- "75% Gore-Tex height" treated as approximate upper bootie-height seam-removal location.
- Footwear type baseline is any lasted footwear upper.

## Confirmed Scope
Novelty search will target footwear upper constructions involving an internal waterproof bootie and inner lining architecture, especially one-piece heel lining configurations that conceal or eliminate seam transitions in heel/collar regions while integrating foam and improving heel fit and comfort.

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

A prior art search was conducted for a footwear upper-inner-lining construction centered on a one-piece heel lining replacing a separate collar-top lining, extending to a waterproof bootie/insole region, integrating padding in the heel/collar region, reducing or relocating liner/bootie seams in the upper heel area, and covering internal rear seams for improved comfort and heel fit. The search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the individual core features and several references covering adjacent combinations, particularly in waterproof bootie integration, seam relocation, continuous inner sleeve constructions, and padded heel/collar structures [US12564246B2; US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; CA3077769A1; EP3654796A1]. No single located reference was found to clearly disclose the entire exact claimed combination as described, but the core feature set is substantially covered across multiple earlier references.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Waterproof bootie/sockliner constructions attached to upper or insole [US12564241B2; US12501960B2; EP3654796A1]
- One-piece or shoe-shaped inner sleeve / inner sock constructions [US11058178B2; US12302989B2]
- Heel/collar lining with integrated pad or foam structures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1; US12520908B2; US12484666B2]
- Rear-seam relocation away from heel center to side regions [US20210093045A1; US20040205982A1]
- Seam covering over waterproof inner sleeve or bootie seams [US12302989B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Final Coverage | Representative References | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | One-piece heel lining pattern | STRONG | US11058178B2, US6367166B1, CA3077769A1 | Continuous/unitary inner liner concepts present, though exact collar-top replacement framing varies |
| F2 | Lining extended to bootie/insole | STRONG | US12564241B2, US12501960B2, EP3654796A1, US12564246B2 | Well covered |
| F3 | Collar foam integrated in heel lining | STRONG | US12564246B2, CA3077769A1, US12520908B2, US12484666B2 | Strong direct and analogous support |
| F4 | Removal/relocation of upper bootie-lining seam | STRONG | US20210093045A1, US12564246B2, US20040205982A1, US12564241B2 | Strongly covered via seam relocation and moved attachment zones |
| F5 | Heel-center bootie seam covered by lining | STRONG | US12302989B2, US12520908B2, US20210093045A1 | Best support from seam-cover and seam-avoidance art; exact “new fully lining” wording not found |
| F6 | Comfort and fit improvement | STRONG | US20210093045A1, US12484666B2, US10314362B2 | Result feature, widely present |
| F7 | Manufacturing approach | STRONG | US12501960B2, US10952502B2, US11484091B2 | Well covered |

### Technology / Prior-Art Themes
- Waterproof breathable footwear increasingly uses internal bootie or inner-sleeve architectures rather than simple loose liners [EP3654796A1; US12564241B2].
- Seam management for comfort is a recurring theme, especially moving seams away from rear heel center and covering seam junctions [US20210093045A1; US12302989B2; US20040205982A1].
- Heel/collar fit is often improved by integrated pads, compressible layers, or heel counter-adjacent foam structures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1; US12484666B2].

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk is **high** from a prior-art coverage perspective because every core feature has at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 support, even though the exact full combination was not located in one single reference [US12564246B2; US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; CA3077769A1; EP3654796A1]. This is a prior-art coverage summary only, not a patentability opinion.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis based on the references identified:

- **F1 (one-piece heel lining pattern):** Earlier references disclose continuous or unitary inner sleeve / inner lining structures formed as one piece from a cut piece or extending substantially full height [US11058178B2; US12302989B2; US6367166B1].
- **F2 (lining extended to bootie/insole):** Earlier references directly disclose waterproof bootie or membrane constructions attached to insole/sole or interfacing with top-end sockliner portions [US12564241B2; US12501960B2; US12564246B2; EP3654796A1].
- **F3 (collar foam integrated in heel lining):** Earlier references disclose collar pads or heel pads integrated with collar lining, sock, or inner lining structures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1; US12520908B2; US12484666B2].
- **F4 (removal/relocation of upper bootie-lining seam):** Earlier references disclose moving liner seams away from rear heel center to medial/lateral positions, or fixing membrane/liner structures below the top edge so that the conventional upper seam location is altered [US20210093045A1; US20040205982A1; US12564241B2].
- **F5 (heel-center bootie seam covered):** Earlier references disclose explicit seam covering over inner-sleeve seams and/or seam avoidance by relocation away from central heel regions [US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; US12520908B2].

Although no single located reference clearly reproduces the exact narrative combination in the same wording, every core feature is covered by at least one independent earlier reference with Y or Y1 support.

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate prior-art risk: High.**

The top anticipating references from a feature-coverage perspective are **US12564246B2**, **US12302989B2**, and **US20210093045A1**. Together with **CA3077769A1** and **EP3654796A1**, they cover the core architecture of a waterproof inner lining/bootie interface, integrated heel/collar padding, seam relocation, and seam coverage [US12564246B2; US12302989B2; US20210093045A1; CA3077769A1; EP3654796A1].

## Limitations

- Search concentrated on patent literature and semantically surfaced references rather than broad academic databases.
- Primarily English-language and translated records were considered.
- Some CN/TW utility-model references were not fully expanded into complete family-text review.
- One detail lookup failed for US10470517B2; adjacent family references were used instead.
- The “75% Gore-Tex height” limitation was treated as approximate, not as a strict dimensional claim.
- No legal patentability, inventive-step, or filing advice is provided.

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

This verdict is based on prior-art coverage, not a legal patentability opinion. Each core feature of the disclosed construction has earlier support in independent references: one-piece or continuous inner sleeve/lining structures [US11058178B2; US12302989B2], waterproof bootie/insole interface constructions [US12564241B2; EP3654796A1], integrated heel/collar padding within inner sock or lining architectures [US12564246B2; CA3077769A1], seam relocation away from the rear heel center [US20210093045A1; US20040205982A1], and explicit seam covering in waterproof inner sleeves [US12302989B2]. **Invention Title**: Power meter equipped with a crank assembly for a human-powered vehicle  
**Domain**: Bicycle / human-powered vehicle drivetrain sensing and cycling power measurement  
**Key Technical Problem**: Enable a crank-based power meter architecture that can operate both in a dual-sided configuration using two crank-arm-mounted meters and in a single-sided configuration using only one crank-arm-mounted meter while still outputting left/right pedalling-force information.  
**Proposed Solution**: A crank-arm power meter detects pedalling force for an individual crank arm and transmits corresponding information. In a dual-sided mode, separate left and right crank-arm power meters each detect force and the detected values are summed or combined to determine total pedalling force. In a single-sided mode, one crank-arm-mounted power meter detects force on only one crank arm, and left/right pedalling force values are then calculated by estimating or generating imaginary force information for the non-instrumented side and transmitting information representative of the calculated bilateral pedalling force.  
**Key Claims**:  
- One power meter design/platform usable with either a single crank arm or a pair of crank arms  
- Dual-meter configuration on left and right crank arms for direct bilateral force detection and combination  
- Single-meter configuration on only one crank arm with calculated/imaginary left-right pedalling force output  
- Transmission of information representing detected and/or calculated pedalling force values  

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults":

1. **What is the primary measured quantity at the crank arm?**  
   Options: (a) pedalling force only, (b) torque/power derived from force and cadence, (c) both force sensing and power output reporting  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) both force sensing and power output reporting** — bicycle crank power meters typically sense force/strain and report derived power-related output

2. **What is the main novelty focus of the invention?**  
   Options: (a) the sensor hardware on the crank arm, (b) the configurable system architecture supporting both dual-sided and single-sided operation, (c) the wireless communication protocol  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) the configurable system architecture supporting both dual-sided and single-sided operation** — the disclosure emphasizes operational flexibility and calculated bilateral output rather than a new sensing element or protocol

3. **How should the “imaginary pedalling force” on the non-instrumented side be interpreted?**  
   Options: (a) estimated as a mirrored/equal counterpart of detected single-side force, (b) algorithmically inferred bilateral force values from single-side measurement, (c) user-calibrated ratio-based estimation  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) algorithmically inferred bilateral force values from single-side measurement** — this is the broadest and most technically neutral reading of the description

4. **What mounting configuration should be assumed for the power meter?**  
   Options: (a) mounted on the crank arm body, (b) integrated with the crank spindle/spider but associated with one crank arm, (c) any crank-assembly-mounted location capable of attributing force to a single crank arm  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) any crank-assembly-mounted location capable of attributing force to a single crank arm** — this avoids over-narrowing the scope before feature definition

5. **What output/transmission assumption should be used for the search scope?**  
   Options: (a) wireless transmission to an external cycle computer, (b) wired or wireless transmission, (c) any transmission/output of representative force information  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) any transmission/output of representative force information** — transmission appears supportive, and a broader assumption is better for novelty searching

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Primary measured quantity | Both force sensing and power output reporting |
| 2 | Main novelty focus | Configurable system architecture supporting both dual-sided and single-sided operation |
| 3 | Meaning of “imaginary pedalling force” | Algorithmically inferred bilateral force values from single-side measurement |
| 4 | Mounting configuration | Any crank-assembly-mounted location attributable to one crank arm |
| 5 | Output/transmission assumption | Any transmission/output of representative force information |

The closest references do not clearly disclose in one reference the ', 'ai_content_full': '## 1. Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

The searched prior art shows dense activity in bicycle crank-arm power meters, including (i) side-specific crank-arm sensing, (ii) dual-sided left/right crank measurement using paired sensors/meters, and (iii) single-sided measurement with estimated total or bilateral output [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2][US9459167B2][US8899110B2]. The closest references do not clearly disclose in one reference the exact combined proposition of a common power-meter architecture that can be deployed either as a paired left/right crank-arm system or as a one-sided system that calculates bilateral left/right pedalling-force information from a single measured crank arm, but several references collectively come close.

**Key Finding:**
- Strong prior art was found for F1 (single crank-arm force detection), F2 (dual-sided paired meter configuration), F3 (single-sided bilateral/total estimation), and F5/F6 (transmission and aggregation).
- The most material concentration of art lies around: paired crank-arm sensing [EP2925595B1][US9459167B2][US8899110B2], single-sided estimation [US9964456B2][US11852558B2], and crank-arm-mounted meter hardware [US10076681B2].
- The principal residual distinction after this search is the specific architecture-level combination expressed as the **same platform/design supporting both one-sided and two-sided crank deployments while outputting bilateral left/right values in the one-sided mode**.

**Types of Solutions Identified:**
1. **Crank-arm-mounted single-side meters** — strain-gauge power meters on one crank arm with transmitted output [US10076681B2][CN206031688U].
2. **Dual-sided left/right crank sensing systems** — separate sensing on both crank arms with bilateral output or work determination [EP2925595B1][US9459167B2][US8899110B2][CN206031683U].
3. **Single-sided estimation systems** — one measured side with asymmetry/weighting-based estimation of total power [US9964456B2][US11852558B2].
4. **Modular/common-hardware crank power modules** — removable or adaptable meter modules, though not necessarily the exact one-side/two-side operational combination [CN213049329U][CN219912594U][EP3144653B1].

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Coverage Level | Main References | Observed Gap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Single crank-arm force detection | SATURATED | US10076681B2, US9459167B2, CN206031688U | No major gap |
| F2 | Dual-sided paired meter configuration | SATURATED | EP2925595B1, US9459167B2, US8899110B2, CN206031683U | No major gap |
| F3 | Single-sided bilateral estimation | STRONG | US9964456B2, US11852558B2, CN206031688U(Y1) | Exact “imaginary left/right pedalling force values” wording not clearly found |
| F4 | Unified meter platform for one or two cranks | STRONG | EP2925595B1(Y1), CN213049329U(Y), CN219912594U(Y), EP3144653B1(Y1) | Exact same product/platform used in both single-meter and dual-meter crank-arm deployments remains less explicit |
| F5 | Transmission of detected/calculated output | SATURATED | US10076681B2, EP2925595B1, US20120330572A1 | No major gap |
| F6 | Combined left-right force aggregation | SATURATED | US9459167B2, US8899110B2, US11852558B2(Y1), CN206031683U | No major gap |

**Technology / Prior-Art Trends:**
- Strong emphasis on **left/right separate measurement** in crank and crank-arm systems.
- Repeated use of **single-sided cost-reduced architectures** that estimate total/bilateral output instead of directly sensing both sides.
- Modern art trends toward **modular power modules**, wireless transmission, and broader compatibility with different crank assemblies.
- Chinese utility-model filings show dense hardware variation around crank, axle, and module packaging.

**Risk Assessment (novelty coverage only):**
- **Aggregate novelty risk: Medium-High**
- Reason: core aspects are individually well represented in earlier references, and some combinations are close. The main remaining distinction appears to lie in how tightly the invention integrates both deployment modes into a single power-meter concept and whether the one-sided mode outputs distinct bilateral left/right values rather than only a corrected total [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2].

| Objective | Assess novelty by searching prior art related to a bicycle/human-powered-vehicle crank power meter usable in both single-crank and paired-crank arrangements |

## 8. Transactional Search Summary (Client-Facing)

**Search Approach**
- Scope: crank-based bicycle/human-powered-vehicle power meter architecture supporting single-side and dual-side usage
- Search methods: keyword-led patent searching plus semantic concept searching
- Focus concepts: crank-arm force sensing, bilateral left/right crank sensing, unilateral estimation of bilateral/total output, modular/common power-meter platform, transmission/output
- Time range: no strict cutoff; references ranged from about 2009 to 2024

**Key Findings**
- Total references retained as relevant: 22
- A-level references: 11
- B-level references: 11
- C-level references noted separately/peripherally: several additional adjacent records not tabulated as primary evidence
- X-category references (all core features clearly Y in one reference): 0 identified in this search set

**Search Outcome Summary**
- The field is crowded for crank-based single-side and dual-side power metering.
- No single retained reference clearly disclosed all of F1-F4 with full Y coverage in one document.
- The closest combined prior-art picture is created by pairing: paired dual-arm architecture [EP2925595B1] with unilateral estimation logic [US9964456B2][US11852558B2].

**Next Steps**
- Full-text legal review of EP2925595B1, US9964456B2, US11852558B2, and related family members for claim-language nuance.
- Consider a family-expansion search around those three references and translation review of the CN single-side/bilateral families.
- Consider searching product manuals / technical white papers if commercial disclosure timing matters.

## Customer Idea
A power meter equipped with a crank assembly for a human-powered vehicle. The power meter can work for both a single crank arm and a pair of crank arms. It detects pedalling force of a single crank arm and transmits information representative of the detected pedalling force. In a dual-sided configuration, one power meter is mounted to a left crank arm and another to a right crank arm, and the detected pedalling forces are summed or combined. In a single-sided configuration, only one power meter is mounted to one of the left/right crank arms, and imaginary or estimated pedalling force of the left and right crank arms is calculated based on the detected pedalling force of the single crank arm, and information representative of the calculated pedalling force is transmitted.

## Clarifications
User requested no scoping questions beyond proposed defaults and asked to proceed directly to scope summary for confirmation.

Confirmed defaults used:
1. Primary measured quantity: both force sensing and power output reporting.
2. Main novelty focus: configurable system architecture supporting both dual-sided and single-sided operation.
3. “Imaginary pedalling force”: algorithmically inferred bilateral force values from single-side measurement.
4. Mounting configuration: any crank-assembly-mounted location attributable to one crank arm.
5. Output/transmission assumption: any transmission/output of representative force information.

## Confirmed Scope
Novelty search will focus on crank-based power meter systems for bicycles or other human-powered vehicles that support: (i) bilateral direct sensing using two crank-arm-associated meters; and/or (ii) unilateral sensing using one crank-arm-associated meter with calculated left/right pedalling force output inferred from the single measured side. The searched prior art shows dense activity in bicycle crank-arm power meters, including (i) side-specific crank-arm sensing, (ii) dual-sided left/right crank measurement using paired sensors/meters, and (iii) single-sided measurement with estimated total or bilateral output [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2][US9459167B2][US8899110B2]. The closest references do not clearly disclose in one reference the exact combined proposition of a common power-meter architecture that can be deployed either as a paired left/right crank-arm system or as a one-sided system that calculates bilateral left/right pedalling-force information from a single measured crank arm, but several references collectively come close.

**Key Finding:**
- Strong prior art was found for F1 (single crank-arm force detection), F2 (dual-sided paired meter configuration), F3 (single-sided bilateral/total estimation), and F5/F6 (transmission and aggregation).
- The most material concentration of art lies around: paired crank-arm sensing [EP2925595B1][US9459167B2][US8899110B2], single-sided estimation [US9964456B2][US11852558B2], and crank-arm-mounted meter hardware [US10076681B2].
- The principal residual distinction after this search is the specific architecture-level combination expressed as the **same platform/design supporting both one-sided and two-sided crank deployments while outputting bilateral left/right values in the one-sided mode**.

**Types of Solutions Identified:**
1. **Crank-arm-mounted single-side meters** — strain-gauge power meters on one crank arm with transmitted output [US10076681B2][CN206031688U].
2. **Dual-sided left/right crank sensing systems** — separate sensing on both crank arms with bilateral output or work determination [EP2925595B1][US9459167B2][US8899110B2][CN206031683U].
3. **Single-sided estimation systems** — one measured side with asymmetry/weighting-based estimation of total power [US9964456B2][US11852558B2].
4. **Modular/common-hardware crank power modules** — removable or adaptable meter modules, though not necessarily the exact one-side/two-side operational combination [CN213049329U][CN219912594U][EP3144653B1].

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Coverage Level | Main References | Observed Gap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Single crank-arm force detection | SATURATED | US10076681B2, US9459167B2, CN206031688U | No major gap |
| F2 | Dual-sided paired meter configuration | SATURATED | EP2925595B1, US9459167B2, US8899110B2, CN206031683U | No major gap |
| F3 | Single-sided bilateral estimation | STRONG | US9964456B2, US11852558B2, CN206031688U(Y1) | Exact “imaginary left/right pedalling force values” wording not clearly found |
| F4 | Unified meter platform for one or two cranks | STRONG | EP2925595B1(Y1), CN213049329U(Y), CN219912594U(Y), EP3144653B1(Y1) | Exact same product/platform used in both single-meter and dual-meter crank-arm deployments remains less explicit |
| F5 | Transmission of detected/calculated output | SATURATED | US10076681B2, EP2925595B1, US20120330572A1 | No major gap |
| F6 | Combined left-right force aggregation | SATURATED | US9459167B2, US8899110B2, US11852558B2(Y1), CN206031683U | No major gap |

**Technology / Prior-Art Trends:**
- Strong emphasis on **left/right separate measurement** in crank and crank-arm systems.
- Repeated use of **single-sided cost-reduced architectures** that estimate total/bilateral output instead of directly sensing both sides.
- Modern art trends toward **modular power modules**, wireless transmission, and broader compatibility with different crank assemblies.
- Chinese utility-model filings show dense hardware variation around crank, axle, and module packaging.

**Risk Assessment (novelty coverage only):**
- **Aggregate novelty risk: Medium-High**
- Reason: core aspects are individually well represented in earlier references, and some combinations are close. The main remaining distinction appears to lie in how tightly the invention integrates both deployment modes into a single power-meter concept and whether the one-sided mode outputs distinct bilateral left/right values rather than only a corrected total [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2].

## 8. Transactional Search Summary (Client-Facing)

**Search Approach**
- Scope: crank-based bicycle/human-powered-vehicle power meter architecture supporting single-side and dual-side usage
- Search methods: keyword-led patent searching plus semantic concept searching
- Focus concepts: crank-arm force sensing, bilateral left/right crank sensing, unilateral estimation of bilateral/total output, modular/common power-meter platform, transmission/output
- Time range: no strict cutoff; references ranged from about 2009 to 2024

**Key Findings**
- Total references retained as relevant: 22
- A-level references: 11
- B-level references: 11
- C-level references noted separately/peripherally: several additional adjacent records not tabulated as primary evidence
- X-category references (all core features clearly Y in one reference): 0 identified in this search set

**Search Outcome Summary**
- The field is crowded for crank-based single-side and dual-side power metering.
- No single retained reference clearly disclosed all of F1-F4 with full Y coverage in one document.
- The closest combined prior-art picture is created by pairing: paired dual-arm architecture [EP2925595B1] with unilateral estimation logic [US9964456B2][US11852558B2].

**Next Steps**
- Full-text legal review of EP2925595B1, US9964456B2, US11852558B2, and related family members for claim-language nuance.
- Consider a family-expansion search around those three references and translation review of the CN single-side/bilateral families.
- Consider searching product manuals / technical white papers if commercial disclosure timing matters.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis:
- **F1 (single crank-arm force detection):** clearly present in multiple earlier references, especially US10076681B2, US9459167B2, CN206031688U, and CA3017924A1 [US10076681B2][US9459167B2][CN206031688U][CA3017924A1].
- **F2 (dual-sided paired meter configuration):** clearly present in earlier references such as EP2925595B1, US9459167B2, US8899110B2, CN206031683U, and CN205483352U [EP2925595B1][US9459167B2][US8899110B2][CN206031683U][CN205483352U].
- **F3 (single-sided bilateral estimation):** earlier references disclose measuring only one side and estimating total/bilateral-equivalent output using asymmetry or weighting, most clearly US9964456B2 and US11852558B2 [US9964456B2][US11852558B2]. What remained less explicit in this search set is wording directed to generating separate “imaginary left and right pedalling force” values from one measured side, as opposed to a corrected total power value.
- **F4 (same platform for one-side or two-side deployment):** earlier references show parts of this concept, including paired multi-mode crank-arm systems [EP2925595B1] and modular/common power-module concepts [CN213049329U][CN219912594U][EP3144653B1]. However, the exact combined teaching of the same crank-arm meter platform operating both as a standalone unilateral meter and as one of a pair of coordinated left/right meters was not found clearly and expressly in one retained document.

On the evidence located, the invention’s core is substantially covered in part by multiple earlier references, but the exact integrated combination of the core features was not clearly found in a single retained reference with full express support across F1-F4 [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2].

**Verdict: partially_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High**

The strongest anticipating cluster is EP2925595B1 for dual crank-arm paired architecture, plus US9964456B2 and US11852558B2 for one-sided measurement with inferred bilateral/total output [EP2925595B1][US9964456B2][US11852558B2]. Even if no single reference in this search set fully expressed the exact same-platform combination, the field is dense and the core pieces were found independently in earlier art.

- Patent-focused search; no exhaustive non-patent literature review completed
- No Japanese/Korean full-language expansion in this round
- Several Chinese records rely on machine-translated abstracts/claims
- No full prosecution-history review or family-complete comparison
- Search was strong on direct crank-arm power meter concepts but not exhaustive for every synonym in sports-performance literature
- Some publication dates and legal-status nuances were not fully extracted for every record

**Verdict: partially_novel**

The remaining potential distinction is the particular integration of these ideas into a single power-meter architecture expressly usable in both one-meter and two-meter crank-arm deployments while producing bilateral left/right output in the one-meter mode. That works better for narrow track widths and lower speeds, but becomes less stable for wider track widths and higher-speed cornering because the inner an', 'ai_content_full': '## Scope Summary

**Invention Title**: Converging Swingarm Steering and Suspension Device

**Domain**: Vehicle dynamics, motorcycle-derived multi-wheel steering/suspension geometry, leaning front-axle mechanisms

**Key Technical Problem**: Conventional leaning motorcycle-based multi-wheel vehicles typically keep the wheels tilting in parallel. That works better for narrow track widths and lower speeds, but becomes less stable for wider track widths and higher-speed cornering because the inner and outer wheels should ideally follow different concentric paths and adopt different lean angles. Existing front-end geometries also risk disrupting suspension/steering parameters such as camber, caster, toe, and scrub radius.

**Proposed Solution**: A front steering and suspension architecture using converging wheel kinematics so that the inner and outer wheels do not tilt in parallel. Instead, each wheel follows its own concentric circular trajectory with a different lean angle, while the wheel centerlines / rotation centers converge toward a common curve center, analogous in effect to Ackermann geometry but adapted to a leaning motorcycle-type front suspension without undermining key front-end geometry constraints.

**Key Claims**:  
- A steering/suspension device for a motorcycle-based multi-wheel vehicle with non-parallel wheel tilt during cornering  
- Inner and outer wheels configured to follow different concentric turning radii  
- Different lean angles for inner and outer wheels during the same turn  
- Convergence of wheel rotation centers toward the turn center  
- A swingarm-based or equivalent suspension geometry that preserves camber, caster, toe, and scrub-radius behavior better than conventional front-axle implementations  
- Suitability for wider-track and higher-speed leaning vehicles

The strongest references collectively cover the core concept of a motorcycle-derived multi-track leaning front end with two front wheels, linked or swingarm-supported steering/suspension members, and inner/outer wheel steering relationships directed toward a common turn center [CN110936785B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2, US11407466B2, IN201741019416A].

The closest references do not all use the exact phrase "converging swingarm," but multiple earlier documents disclose equivalent or closely adjacent mechanics: (i) dual-front-wheel leaning architectures [US10583885B2, US11136087B2, US12491962B2, EP3059151B1], (ii) swingarm or upper/lower arm linked suspensions [IN201741019416A, CN216401638U, US12589824B2], (iii) non-parallel and/or differential wheel geometric behavior during lean/turn events [US11407466B2, CN109353406B], and (iv) steering-axis convergence / turn-center formulations associated with Ackermann or projected intersection geometry [CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Tilting forecarriage / articulated quadrilateral systems**: Piaggio-type front modules with paired front wheels and articulated upright support [US10583885B2, US20180327044A1, US20180222527A1].
- **Dual-arm / swingarm leaning front modules**: Kawasaki, Yamaha, Honda, Arcimoto, Orxa, and related families using upper/lower arms, parallel links, or dual swingarms [US11511821B2, US12491962B2, US11136087B2, US11407466B2, IN201741019416A, EP3059151B1].
- **Ackermann side-tilting steering systems**: Chinese side-roll/side-tilting mechanisms explicitly stating outer/inner wheel deflection angles satisfy Ackermann conditions [CN110936785B, CN109353406B].
- **Geometry-control references**: patents emphasizing toe/camber/caster/kingpin/scrub-style coordination or projected steering-axis relationships [US8911332B2, US8535206B2, US20180327044A1, US9238395B2].

### Technology / Prior-Art Trends
The prior art is dense in reverse trikes, leaning three-wheelers, side-tilting chassis, articulated quadrilateral forecarriages, and multi-link front suspension modules. Several families focus on preserving steering feel, camber/toe behavior, or projected steering-axis geometry while enabling lean [US8911332B2, US20180327044A1, US12097926B2]. Ackermann-like adaptation to tilting/side-roll platforms appears explicitly in Chinese filings [CN110936785B, CN109353406B].

## Novelty Assessment

The search evidence indicates that the claimed concept is heavily covered at the feature level by earlier independent references. The closest single references do not all recite the identical phraseology used in the invention summary, but the prior art clearly teaches the major feature groups separately and often in overlapping combinations.

- **F1 (front leaning multi-wheel architecture)** is directly present in numerous earlier references involving reverse trikes, rolling type vehicles, and dual-front-wheel leaning vehicles [US10583885B2, US11136087B2, US12491962B2, US11511821B2, EP3059151B1].
- **F2 (non-parallel wheel tilt / differential wheel attitude)** is taught or strongly implied by references describing increasing camber between paired front wheels or inward wheel-group inclination during turning [US11407466B2, CN216401638U, CN109353406B].
- **F3 (concentric turning radii geometry)** is not always phrased as “concentric circles,” but earlier references disclose functionally equivalent turn-center / Ackermann geometry formulations: inner/outer wheel deflection relationships satisfying Ackermann conditions and wheel/support-member motion around a turning center [CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US11511821B2, US20180327044A1].
- **F4 (common curve-center convergence)** is strongly shown by projected steering-axis intersection geometry and turn-center language [US20180327044A1, US11511821B2, CN110936785B].
- **F5 (swingarm-linked front suspension mechanism)** is directly disclosed in dual swingarm, upper/lower arm, fork-arm, and articulated-quadrilateral front-end references [IN201741019416A, CN216401638U, US12589824B2, US10583885B2].
- **F6 (preservation of camber/caster/toe/scrub geometry)** is addressed by geometry-control references emphasizing toe/camber/caster/KPI relationships, unchanged tyre plane, or controlled steering-axis behavior [US8911332B2, US8535206B2, IN201741019416A, US12097926B2].
- **F8 (front-axle Ackermann adaptation for leaning vehicle)** is explicitly present in side-tilting steering references [CN110936785B, CN109353406B], and strongly supported by projected steering-axis convergence in Piaggio’s forecarriage [US20180327044A1].

Because every core feature is covered by at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 support, and several references are very close on the same overall combination, the prior-art coverage points to a non-novel outcome in this search framework.

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The strongest anticipating/closest references are **CN110936785B**, **US20180327044A1**, and **US11511821B2**. Together with supporting geometry-control and swingarm references such as **IN201741019416A** and **US8911332B2**, they create a dense prior-art field around the disclosed concept. This is an evidence-based novelty risk summary only, not a legal patentability opinion.

## Limitations

- Search emphasized patent literature over non-patent literature because the strongest relevant evidence was patent-dense.
- Some Chinese and Japanese references were evaluated from machine-translated abstracts/claims and DWPI-style summaries.
- Exact legal status, family completeness, and prosecution file histories were not exhaustively checked.
- No attorney analysis of claim construction, inventive step, enablement, or patentability was performed.
- The user disclosure lacked formal claims, so feature decomposition was based on the confirmed scope and disclosed technical narrative.

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

Rationale: earlier references independently disclose all core aspects of the concept at least partially, and several references disclose them in closely overlapping combinations. In particular, CN110936785B supplies explicit Ackermann-condition language in a side-tilting front mechanism, US20180327044A1 supplies projected steering-axis convergence toward a common support point, US11511821B2 supplies explicit turning-center motion of both front wheels in a leaning parallel-link architecture, and IN201741019416A supplies dual swingarm plus tire-plane-preservation concepts. Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

A concentrated prior-art cluster was identified around leaning dual-front-wheel vehicle architectures, front swingarm/parallel-link/forecarriage suspensions, and Ackermann-like steering adaptation in tilting vehicles. The strongest references collectively cover the core concept of a motorcycle-derived multi-track leaning front end with two front wheels, linked or swingarm-supported steering/suspension members, and inner/outer wheel steering relationships directed toward a common turn center [CN110936785B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2, US11407466B2, IN201741019416A].

The closest references do not all use the exact phrase "converging swingarm," but multiple earlier documents disclose equivalent or closely adjacent mechanics: (i) dual-front-wheel leaning architectures [US10583885B2, US11136087B2, US12491962B2, EP3059151B1], (ii) swingarm or upper/lower arm linked suspensions [IN201741019416A, CN216401638U, US12589824B2], (iii) non-parallel and/or differential wheel geometric behavior during lean/turn events [US11407466B2, CN109353406B], and (iv) steering-axis convergence / turn-center formulations associated with Ackermann or projected intersection geometry [CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Tilting forecarriage / articulated quadrilateral systems**: Piaggio-type front modules with paired front wheels and articulated upright support [US10583885B2, US20180327044A1, US20180222527A1].
- **Dual-arm / swingarm leaning front modules**: Kawasaki, Yamaha, Honda, Arcimoto, Orxa, and related families using upper/lower arms, parallel links, or dual swingarms [US11511821B2, US12491962B2, US11136087B2, US11407466B2, IN201741019416A, EP3059151B1].
- **Ackermann side-tilting steering systems**: Chinese side-roll/side-tilting mechanisms explicitly stating outer/inner wheel deflection angles satisfy Ackermann conditions [CN110936785B, CN109353406B].
- **Geometry-control references**: patents emphasizing toe/camber/caster/kingpin/scrub-style coordination or projected steering-axis relationships [US8911332B2, US8535206B2, US20180327044A1, US9238395B2].

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Final Coverage | Representative References | Gap Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Front leaning multi-wheel architecture | SATURATED | US10583885B2, US11136087B2, US12491962B2, US11511821B2, EP3059151B1 | No material gap |
| F2 | Non-parallel wheel tilt kinematics | STRONG | US11407466B2, CN216401638U, CN110936785B, CN109353406B | No material gap |
| F3 | Concentric turning radii geometry | STRONG | CN110936785B, US11511821B2, US20180327044A1, CN109353406B | Covered through Ackermann / turn-center / projected-intersection formulations |
| F4 | Common curve-center convergence | STRONG | US20180327044A1, CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US11511821B2 | No material gap |
| F5 | Swingarm-linked front suspension mechanism | SATURATED | IN201741019416A, US12589824B2, CN216401638U, US10583885B2 | No material gap |
| F6 | Preservation of camber/caster/toe/scrub geometry | STRONG | US8911332B2, US8535206B2, IN201741019416A, US12097926B2 | No material gap |
| F7 | Wide-track high-speed stability objective | MODERATE | CN115520275A, US8535206B2, US12528557B2 | Non-core; adequate support |
| F8 | Front-axle Ackermann adaptation for leaning vehicle | STRONG | CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2 | No material gap |

### Technology / Prior-Art Trends
The prior art is dense in reverse trikes, leaning three-wheelers, side-tilting chassis, articulated quadrilateral forecarriages, and multi-link front suspension modules. Several families focus on preserving steering feel, camber/toe behavior, or projected steering-axis geometry while enabling lean [US8911332B2, US20180327044A1, US12097926B2]. Ackermann-like adaptation to tilting/side-roll platforms appears explicitly in Chinese filings [CN110936785B, CN109353406B].

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk is **high** from a prior-art coverage standpoint because every core feature has at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 coverage, and several references together form a dense mosaic around the claimed architecture [CN110936785B, US20180327044A1, US11511821B2, IN201741019416A, US8911332B2]. This is a prior-art coverage summary only, not a legal patentability opinion.

|---|---|
| Objective | Assess novelty of a converging swingarm steering and suspension device for a motorcycle-derived leaning multi-wheel vehicle using prior-art search |
| In-Scope | Mechanical front steering/suspension geometry; paired front wheels; leaning/tilting architectures; Ackermann-like convergence; swingarm/link mechanisms; geometry-preservation features |
| Out-of-Scope | Seat monitoring, driver behavior tracking, gamification text; trademark/FTO/legal opinions; rear-axle-only systems unless used as analogical geometry support |
| Authorities | Patent literature primarily; some semantically surfaced NPL-like concepts were screened, but patent literature dominated relevant evidence |
| Languages | English and machine-translated Chinese/Japanese/other patent records |
| Known Constraints | Disclosure contained unrelated seat/driver-monitoring text treated as erroneous insertion; no formal claims provided by user |
| Open Questions | Exact proprietary linkage dimensions and whether the user’s implementation uses explicit differential wheel lean vs equivalent differential steering/turn-center geometry |
| Assumptions | Front axle focus; leaning three-wheeler/multi-track motorcycle-derived vehicle; mechanical geometry only; dual-front linked/swingarm interpretation |
| Feature Confirmation | Gate 2 confirmed with features F1-F8 as set out in /features.md |

## Novelty Assessment

The search evidence indicates that the claimed concept is heavily covered at the feature level by earlier independent references. The closest single references do not all recite the identical phraseology used in the invention summary, but the prior art clearly teaches the major feature groups separately and often in overlapping combinations.

- **F1 (front leaning multi-wheel architecture)** is directly present in numerous earlier references involving reverse trikes, rolling type vehicles, and dual-front-wheel leaning vehicles [US10583885B2, US11136087B2, US12491962B2, US11511821B2, EP3059151B1].
- **F2 (non-parallel wheel tilt / differential wheel attitude)** is taught or strongly implied by references describing increasing camber between paired front wheels or inward wheel-group inclination during turning [US11407466B2, CN216401638U, CN109353406B].
- **F3 (concentric turning radii geometry)** is not always phrased as “concentric circles,” but earlier references disclose functionally equivalent turn-center / Ackermann geometry formulations: inner/outer wheel deflection relationships satisfying Ackermann conditions and wheel/support-member motion around a turning center [CN110936785B, CN109353406B, US11511821B2, US20180327044A1].
- **F4 (common curve-center convergence)** is strongly shown by projected steering-axis intersection geometry and turn-center language [US20180327044A1, US11511821B2, CN110936785B].
- **F5 (swingarm-linked front suspension mechanism)** is directly disclosed in dual swingarm, upper/lower arm, fork-arm, and articulated-quadrilateral front-end references [IN201741019416A, CN216401638U, US12589824B2, US10583885B2].
- **F6 (preservation of camber/caster/toe/scrub geometry)** is addressed by geometry-control references emphasizing toe/camber/caster/KPI relationships, unchanged tyre plane, or controlled steering-axis behavior [US8911332B2, US8535206B2, IN201741019416A, US12097926B2].
- **F8 (front-axle Ackermann adaptation for leaning vehicle)** is explicitly present in side-tilting steering references [CN110936785B, CN109353406B], and strongly supported by projected steering-axis convergence in Piaggio’s forecarriage [US20180327044A1].

Because every core feature is covered by at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 support, and several references are very close on the same overall combination, the prior-art coverage points to a non-novel outcome in this search framework.

**Verdict: not_novel**

**Invention Title**: Noninvasive Smartphone-Based Hemoglobin Estimation Through Combined Visual and Physiological Signal Processing  
**Domain**: Noninvasive biomedical sensing / mobile health / optical hemoglobin monitoring  
**Key Technical Problem**: Existing hemoglobin measurement approaches are invasive or insufficiently accurate in noninvasive form.  
**Proposed Solution**: A fingertip hemoglobin monitor using a clip-style body with a top opening and a bottom section carrying a light source and heart-rate sensor, arranged so transmitted/reflected light and physiological signals from the nail-bed region can be captured and processed, with intended smartphone-based estimation of hemoglobin.  
**Key Claims**:  
- A clip-on fingertip device architecture with a shaped top opening and lower sensor/light arrangement  
- Optical illumination through the fingertip/nail-bed region  
- Inclusion of an HR sensor alongside the light source  
- Smartphone-based hemoglobin estimation using combined visual and physiological signal processing  
- Noninvasive hemoglobin estimation from fingertip sensing

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults":

1. **Is the invention being assessed primarily as (a) a dedicated clip hardware device, (b) a smartphone-assisted accessory system, or (c) a pure smartphone-app method?**  
   Options: (a) dedicated clip hardware device, (b) smartphone-assisted accessory system, (c) pure smartphone-app method  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) smartphone-assisted accessory system** — the title emphasizes smartphone-based estimation, while the body text describes a physical clip device.

2. **Is the light path assumed to be mainly (a) transmissive through the fingertip, (b) reflective from the nail-bed/fingertip tissue, or (c) either optical mode depending on implementation?**  
   Options: (a) transmissive through the fingertip, (b) reflective from the nail-bed/fingertip tissue, (c) either optical mode depending on implementation  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) either optical mode depending on implementation** — the text suggests light passing through the open section toward the fingertip area, but does not fully limit the sensing geometry.

3. **What is the HR sensor most reasonably interpreted as?**  
   Options: (a) photoplethysmography sensor, (b) contact pulse sensor, (c) generic heart-rate sensing module  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) photoplethysmography sensor** — this is the most common fingertip-compatible heart-rate sensing approach and aligns with optical hemoglobin monitoring.

4. **Should the shaped top opening geometry be treated as a core novelty aspect or primarily a supporting structural detail?**  
   Options: (a) core novelty aspect, (b) supporting structural detail, (c) uncertain but include for search  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) uncertain but include for search** — the geometry is described specifically enough to search, but it may be ancillary to the sensing concept.

5. **Should the search focus on hemoglobin estimation specifically, or also include adjacent analyte-estimation prior art?**  
   Options: (a) hemoglobin only, (b) hemoglobin + anemia screening, (c) broader blood/analyte optical estimation systems  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) hemoglobin + anemia screening** — anemia-screening prior art is highly likely to overlap materially with hemoglobin-estimation systems.

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | System type | (b) smartphone-assisted accessory system |
| 2 | Optical mode | (c) either transmissive or reflective |
| 3 | HR sensor type | (a) photoplethysmography sensor |
| 4 | Opening geometry role | (c) uncertain but include for search |
| 5 | Search breadth | (b) hemoglobin + anemia screening |

Additional earlier references cover the structural finger-clip / finger-probe architect', 'ai_content_full': '## 1. Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

The prior art search identified multiple earlier references covering the invention’s core concept of noninvasive hemoglobin estimation using fingertip optical sensing, physiological pulse-related signal extraction, and mobile/smartphone-assisted processing [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; US11243216B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. Additional earlier references cover the structural finger-clip / finger-probe architecture and retention mechanisms for optical physiological sensing at the finger [US12514503B2; US12245852B2; US20070060807A1; US6213952B1].

The strongest concentration of prior art lies around: (i) smartphone or handheld noninvasive hemoglobin prediction from finger imagery and extracted PPG cycles; (ii) noninvasive hemoglobin prediction from optical pulse-wave features and regression/learning models; and (iii) finger-mounted or clip-type optical probe structures with LEDs, detectors, hinges, wraps, or opposing finger-contact portions.

The most weakly covered aspect is the exact geometry of the top opening—specifically an opening with an oval top end, parallel sides, and flat bottom end. That feature appears to be a narrower structural detail rather than the center of the disclosed sensing concept. The search found only adjacent window/aperture/cavity references, not a strong earlier disclosure of that exact geometry in the same hemoglobin-monitor context [US12419549B2; US11684297B2; US20150112169A1].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Smartphone/handheld hemoglobin prediction from finger video or image data
- Noninvasive hemoglobin estimation from PPG / photoelectric volume pulse-wave signals
- Finger-mounted optical probes and clip sensors for physiological monitoring
- Nail-side / dorsal-side illumination arrangements for through-finger optical analysis
- Multi-feature predictive models combining optical and physiological features

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Final Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Fingertip clip hemoglobin monitor | STRONG | Structural finger-clip / finger-probe art exists, and hemoglobin-specific optical finger-probe art also exists [US12514503B2; US12245852B2; IN202411007499A]. |
| F2 | Smartphone-assisted estimation | STRONG | Directly covered by smartphone/handheld hemoglobin references [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; US11243216B2]. |
| F3 | Combined optical and HR sensing | STRONG | Covered by PPG/image-derived pulse extraction and photoelectric pulse-wave references [US12089930B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. |
| F4 | Nail-bed directed optical geometry | STRONG | Covered at least partially by dorsal-side illumination / ventral pad-tip / fingernail-facing probe arrangements [US12089930B2; IN202411007499A]. |
| F5 | Shaped top opening structure | MODERATE | Only adjacent aperture/window/cavity references found; exact geometry not clearly located. |
| F6 | Clip biasing mechanism | STRONG | Earlier clip, hinge, wrap, and force-applying retention structures found [US12245852B2; US12514503B2; US6213952B1]. |
| F7 | Hemoglobin estimation from visual and physiological signal processing | STRONG | Strongly covered by multimodal predictive-model prior art [US12089930B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. |

### Technology Trends
The search results show a dense prior-art field in noninvasive blood-parameter estimation using optical sensing, especially from pulse-wave signals and image-derived PPG. A separate but overlapping cluster exists around smartphone-based screening and handheld medical accessories. Structural finger-clip and finger-probe optical hardware is also mature and longstanding, with many references in adjacent oximetry and blood-parameter sensing.

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk is **high** from a prior-art coverage standpoint because all core features are covered by earlier references individually, and several high-relevance references disclose substantial overlap with the central system concept [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; CN114869276A; CN117462121A].

## Customer Idea
Noninvasive smartphone-based hemoglobin estimation through combined visual and physiological signal processing. The disclosure describes a fingertip hemoglobin monitor device with a clip-style body, a top portion having an open section with an oval top end, parallel sides, and flat bottom end, and a bottom portion comprising a light source and an HR sensor. A fingertip is placed between top and bottom portions with the nail bed facing the open section.

## Clarifications

- Treat as a smartphone-assisted accessory system.

- HR sensor assumed to be a photoplethysmography sensor.
- Opening geometry included in search but treated as uncertain core significance.
- Search breadth includes hemoglobin estimation and anemia screening.

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

The prior art search identified multiple earlier references covering the invention’s core concept of noninvasive hemoglobin estimation using fingertip optical sensing, physiological pulse-related signal extraction, and mobile/smartphone-assisted processing [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; US11243216B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. Additional earlier references cover the structural finger-clip / finger-probe architecture and retention mechanisms for optical physiological sensing at the finger [US12514503B2; US12245852B2; US20070060807A1; US6213952B1].

The strongest concentration of prior art lies around: (i) smartphone or handheld noninvasive hemoglobin prediction from finger imagery and extracted PPG cycles; (ii) noninvasive hemoglobin prediction from optical pulse-wave features and regression/learning models; and (iii) finger-mounted or clip-type optical probe structures with LEDs, detectors, hinges, wraps, or opposing finger-contact portions.

The most weakly covered aspect is the exact geometry of the top opening—specifically an opening with an oval top end, parallel sides, and flat bottom end. That feature appears to be a narrower structural detail rather than the center of the disclosed sensing concept. The search found only adjacent window/aperture/cavity references, not a strong earlier disclosure of that exact geometry in the same hemoglobin-monitor context [US12419549B2; US11684297B2; US20150112169A1].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Smartphone/handheld hemoglobin prediction from finger video or image data
- Noninvasive hemoglobin estimation from PPG / photoelectric volume pulse-wave signals
- Finger-mounted optical probes and clip sensors for physiological monitoring
- Nail-side / dorsal-side illumination arrangements for through-finger optical analysis
- Multi-feature predictive models combining optical and physiological features

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Description | Final Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Fingertip clip hemoglobin monitor | STRONG | Structural finger-clip / finger-probe art exists, and hemoglobin-specific optical finger-probe art also exists [US12514503B2; US12245852B2; IN202411007499A]. |
| F2 | Smartphone-assisted estimation | STRONG | Directly covered by smartphone/handheld hemoglobin references [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; US11243216B2]. |
| F3 | Combined optical and HR sensing | STRONG | Covered by PPG/image-derived pulse extraction and photoelectric pulse-wave references [US12089930B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. |
| F4 | Nail-bed directed optical geometry | STRONG | Covered at least partially by dorsal-side illumination / ventral pad-tip / fingernail-facing probe arrangements [US12089930B2; IN202411007499A]. |
| F5 | Shaped top opening structure | MODERATE | Only adjacent aperture/window/cavity references found; exact geometry not clearly located. |
| F6 | Clip biasing mechanism | STRONG | Earlier clip, hinge, wrap, and force-applying retention structures found [US12245852B2; US12514503B2; US6213952B1]. |
| F7 | Hemoglobin estimation from visual and physiological signal processing | STRONG | Strongly covered by multimodal predictive-model prior art [US12089930B2; CN114869276A; CN117462121A]. |

### Technology Trends
The search results show a dense prior-art field in noninvasive blood-parameter estimation using optical sensing, especially from pulse-wave signals and image-derived PPG. A separate but overlapping cluster exists around smartphone-based screening and handheld medical accessories. Structural finger-clip and finger-probe optical hardware is also mature and longstanding, with many references in adjacent oximetry and blood-parameter sensing.

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk is **high** from a prior-art coverage standpoint because all core features are covered by earlier references individually, and several high-relevance references disclose substantial overlap with the central system concept [US12089930B2; WO2019173283A1; CN114869276A; CN117462121A].

## 2. Scope

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| Objective | Assess novelty through prior-art search for a noninvasive smartphone-assisted fingertip hemoglobin monitor using optical and physiological signal processing. |
| In-Scope | Finger clip/accessory architecture; smartphone-assisted hemoglobin estimation; optical sensing; heart-rate/PPG sensing; nail-bed/fingertip geometry; signal fusion. |
| Out-of-Scope | Invasive blood analyzers; general pulse oximeters with no hemoglobin estimation; broad wellness apps unrelated to hemoglobin sensing. |
| Authorities | Patent literature and surfaced non-patent-style analogous concepts from search results. |
| Languages | Primarily English-language patent family records; some Chinese, Korean, and Indian publications via translated metadata. |
| Known Constraints | Search relied on available patent metadata and full-text lookups for selected A/B references; not every family member was expanded. |
| Open Questions | Exact role of the shaped top opening remains uncertain as a core vs. secondary feature. |
| Assumptions | Smartphone-assisted accessory system; PPG-style HR sensing; transmissive and/or reflective optical mode; hemoglobin and anemia screening both considered relevant. |
| Feature Confirmation | User confirmed defaults and confirmed the Feature Matrix prior to research. |
