Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

A prior-art search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the invention’s core concept of a heated aerosol/heated tobacco article using an internal or article-integrated tubular or hollow cooling structure to define aerosol flow and reduce user-facing temperature, together with multiple independent references teaching thermally active, heat-absorbing, heat-storage, or endothermic materials in the same general technical space [US4924886A; US12514281B2; US12543778B2; JP07747719B2; CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CN112369661A; KR2024101197A].

The closest structural references include a cylindrical tube disposed longitudinally through an aerosol-forming substrate [US12543778B2], an annular tube cooling segment defining a chamber for flow and cooling of volatilized material [US12514281B2], a hollow cooling rod with an inner cooling material layer [JP07747719B2], and a rigid tube concentrically located within a tobacco column with heat-absorbing porous material disposed within the tube [US4924886A]. Separately, strong material-function references teach heat storage materials, high heat capacity, and absorption of heat during use in heated aerosol articles [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; RU2757570C2]. A numeric specific-heat threshold above 1.4 kJ/kg·K was also found in a heated cartridge context [JP07818835B2].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Internal tube or hollow channel through tobacco/aerosol substrate [US12543778B2; US4924886A]
- Annular tube / cooling segment downstream of smokable material [US12514281B2]
- Hollow cooling rod / cooling nozzle structures with cooling material layers or porous media [JP07747719B2; CN112369661A]
- Extruded aerosol-cooling elements with internal pathways [US12599164B2]
- Heat diffusers using heat-storage or high-heat-capacity porous bodies [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CA3014148A1; RU2757570C2]
- Tube-shaped cooling structures containing endothermic material [KR2024101197A]
- Heated cartridge sections using materials with specific heat capacity of 1.5–2.5 kJ/(kg·°C) [JP07818835B2]

### Technology/Competitor Trends
The search landscape shows repeated emphasis on: (i) hollow cooling structures, (ii) internal channels through aerosol-generating components, (iii) heat-storage/porous thermal elements, and (iv) cooling sections placed between heated substrate and mouth end. The most active assignee clusters observed in the most relevant set were Altria, Philip Morris / related family records, British American Tobacco, KT&G, and China Tobacco-related entities [US12543778B2; CA3014149A1; US12514281B2; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A].

### Risk Assessment
**Novelty Risk: High**

This is a prior-art coverage summary, not a patentability opinion. The core feature combination is substantially covered by multiple earlier references when read together, and some references come close to covering the combination in one disclosure, especially internal tube + heat-absorbing material [US4924886A] and hollow cooling rod + cooling material layer [JP07747719B2].

### Immediate Recommendations
- Review the closest combination references internally, especially: US4924886A, US12543778B2, JP07747719B2, CN112369661A, KR2024101197A, and JP07818835B2.
- If internal engineering distinctions exist, compare them specifically against: (i) exact placement of tube, (ii) exact material identity, and (iii) whether heat absorption is in the tube body itself or in a layer/porous medium associated with the tube.

### Optional Extended Search

- Extend to older cigarette cooling/filter literature for additional pre-HNB analogs.

### Monitoring

### Prototype Validation
- Internally test whether the proposed material threshold (>1.4 kJ/kg·K) yields a measurable and distinct mouth-end temperature reduction relative to PLA, paper, or standard cooling-tube materials already shown in prior art.

### Market Analysis
That is outside my scope. I specialize in novelty assessment through prior art search.

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Quality: Strong patent coverage across two search rounds with structure-, function-, and material-focused searching plus citation expansion.
- Limitations: Some foreign-language references were assessed through translated abstracts/claims rather than full native-language prosecution histories; legal status not assessed; no FTO analysis performed.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis indicates that the core invention concept is substantially covered in the identified prior art. Internal or article-integrated tubular / hollow cooling structures are clearly present in earlier records [US12543778B2; US12514281B2; JP07747719B2; US4924886A]. Heat-absorbing, heat-storage, endothermic, or high-heat-capacity materials are also clearly present in the same field [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CN112369661A; KR2024101197A]. A numeric specific heat threshold above 1.4 kJ/kg·K is expressly recited in a heated cartridge context [JP07818835B2].

No single identified reference cleanly mapped all confirmed core features as Y under the user-confirmed feature framing, mainly because exact placement “within the aerosol-forming rod” and explicit “high-heat-capacity material of the tubular component itself” are sometimes split across references. However, every core feature has at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 coverage, and several references come close to the combined concept [US4924886A; US12543778B2; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A].

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The search located several earlier references that substantially overlap the core structure/function combination, especially internal/hollow cooling structures and thermally active materials [US4924886A; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A]. The top three anticipating references for practical comparison are US4924886A, US12543778B2, and JP07747719B2 because together they bracket the key combination of internal tube, flow path, and thermally active cooling structure.

## Limitations

- Patent-focused search; non-patent literature was not expanded where patent coverage was already dense
- Some foreign-language records were evaluated using translated claims/abstracts rather than full native-language specifications
- Legal status, prosecution history, and family deduplication beyond identified key members were not exhaustively analyzed
- No FTO, infringement, or patentability analysis performed
- Search date limited to records available as of this assessment session

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

A prior-art search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the invention’s core concept of a heated aerosol/heated tobacco article using an internal or article-integrated tubular or hollow cooling structure to define aerosol flow and reduce user-facing temperature, together with multiple independent references teaching thermally active, heat-absorbing, heat-storage, or endothermic materials in the same general technical space [US4924886A; US12514281B2; US12543778B2; JP07747719B2; CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CN112369661A; KR2024101197A].

The closest structural references include a cylindrical tube disposed longitudinally through an aerosol-forming substrate [US12543778B2], an annular tube cooling segment defining a chamber for flow and cooling of volatilized material [US12514281B2], a hollow cooling rod with an inner cooling material layer [JP07747719B2], and a rigid tube concentrically located within a tobacco column with heat-absorbing porous material disposed within the tube [US4924886A]. Separately, strong material-function references teach heat storage materials, high heat capacity, and absorption of heat during use in heated aerosol articles [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; RU2757570C2]. A numeric specific-heat threshold above 1.4 kJ/kg·K was also found in a heated cartridge context [JP07818835B2].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- Internal tube or hollow channel through tobacco/aerosol substrate [US12543778B2; US4924886A]
- Annular tube / cooling segment downstream of smokable material [US12514281B2]
- Hollow cooling rod / cooling nozzle structures with cooling material layers or porous media [JP07747719B2; CN112369661A]
- Extruded aerosol-cooling elements with internal pathways [US12599164B2]
- Heat diffusers using heat-storage or high-heat-capacity porous bodies [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CA3014148A1; RU2757570C2]
- Tube-shaped cooling structures containing endothermic material [KR2024101197A]
- Heated cartridge sections using materials with specific heat capacity of 1.5–2.5 kJ/(kg·°C) [JP07818835B2]

### Gap Analysis Table
| Feature | Final Coverage | Key References | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 Heated aerosol article format | STRONG | US12514281B2, US12543778B2, US11969007B2 | Well-covered |
| F2 Aerosol-forming rod at heating end | STRONG | US12543778B2, US12582154B2, RU2757570C2 | Well-covered |

### Technology/Competitor Trends
The search landscape shows repeated emphasis on: (i) hollow cooling structures, (ii) internal channels through aerosol-generating components, (iii) heat-storage/porous thermal elements, and (iv) cooling sections placed between heated substrate and mouth end. The most active assignee clusters observed in the most relevant set were Altria, Philip Morris / related family records, British American Tobacco, KT&G, and China Tobacco-related entities [US12543778B2; CA3014149A1; US12514281B2; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A].

### Risk Assessment
**Novelty Risk: High**

This is a prior-art coverage summary, not a patentability opinion. The core feature combination is substantially covered by multiple earlier references when read together, and some references come close to covering the combination in one disclosure, especially internal tube + heat-absorbing material [US4924886A] and hollow cooling rod + cooling material layer [JP07747719B2].

- QA Checklist: Core features searched; semantic expansion used; full-text details retrieved for key A/B references included in feature matrix; report persisted.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis indicates that the core invention concept is substantially covered in the identified prior art. Internal or article-integrated tubular / hollow cooling structures are clearly present in earlier records [US12543778B2; US12514281B2; JP07747719B2; US4924886A]. Heat-absorbing, heat-storage, endothermic, or high-heat-capacity materials are also clearly present in the same field [CA3014149A1; US11969007B2; CN112369661A; KR2024101197A]. A numeric specific heat threshold above 1.4 kJ/kg·K is expressly recited in a heated cartridge context [JP07818835B2].

No single identified reference cleanly mapped all confirmed core features as Y under the user-confirmed feature framing, mainly because exact placement “within the aerosol-forming rod” and explicit “high-heat-capacity material of the tubular component itself” are sometimes split across references. However, every core feature has at least one earlier independent reference with Y or Y1 coverage, and several references come close to the combined concept [US4924886A; US12543778B2; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A].

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The search located several earlier references that substantially overlap the core structure/function combination, especially internal/hollow cooling structures and thermally active materials [US4924886A; JP07747719B2; CN112369661A]. The top three anticipating references for practical comparison are US4924886A, US12543778B2, and JP07747719B2 because together they bracket the key combination of internal tube, flow path, and thermally active cooling structure.

## Limitations

- Patent-focused search; non-patent literature was not expanded where patent coverage was already dense
- Some foreign-language records were evaluated using translated claims/abstracts rather than full native-language specifications
- Legal status, prosecution history, and family deduplication beyond identified key members were not exhaustively analyzed
- No FTO, infringement, or patentability analysis performed
- Search date limited to records available as of this assessment session

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

The identified prior art provides substantial earlier coverage of the invention’s core technical elements. Earlier references disclose internal or hollow cooling tubes/rods, tube-defined airflow passages, heat-absorbing or heat-storage materials, and article structures intended to cool aerosol or smoke before inhalation [US4924886A; US12543778B2; US12514281B2; JP07747719B2; CA3014149A1; CN112369661A]. **Invention Title**: Recycled-Plastic Combined Rebar Chair and Locking Tie  
**Domain**: Concrete reinforcement accessories / rebar support and tie hardware  
**Key Technical Problem**: Existing rebar support systems such as wire-loop ties, plastic chairs, and locking wheel chairs each provide only part of the needed functionality, creating limitations in support, positioning, and secure retention of intersecting or stacked rebar.  
**Proposed Solution**: A two-part rebar tie apparatus with a lower tie portion and an upper tie portion that join together to form a rebar cavity for receiving two stacked, nonparallel rebar rods. The lower portion provides ground support and a seat for the lower rod; the upper portion provides a saddle for the upper rod; and a locking mechanism maintains engagement with both rods after assembly.  
**Key Claims**:  
- Two-piece tie apparatus with lower and upper portions attachable together  
- Rebar cavity configured for two stacked rods  
- Lower portion mountable on ground surface  
- First opening for a lower rod and second opening for an upper rod  
- Lower internal seat abutting the lower rod  
- Upper internal saddle pressing the upper rod  
- Locking mechanism maintaining retained engagement of both rods  
- Use of recycled plastic as the apparent intended material platform

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults":

1. **What material scope should be assumed for the apparatus?**  
   Options: (a) predominantly recycled plastic, (b) any plastic, (c) mixed-material assembly, (d) material unspecified  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) predominantly recycled plastic** — this is stated in the technical field and appears to be part of the commercial/inventive framing

2. **How should the “pair of stacked rebar rods” relationship be interpreted?**  
   Options: (a) vertically separated rods crossing at an angle, (b) parallel stacked rods, (c) rods merely adjacent within one holder, (d) any two rods regardless of orientation  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) vertically separated rods crossing at an angle** — the disclosure says the upper rod is over and nonparallel to the lower rod

3. **What type of attachment between upper and lower tie portions should be assumed?**  
   Options: (a) integral snap-fit or latch connection, (b) hinged closure, (c) separate fastener-based connection, (d) any attachable connection  
   → **Default if confirmed: (d) any attachable connection** — the disclosure requires attachability but does not limit the joining mechanism

4. **What should be assumed about the locking mechanism?**  
   Options: (a) mechanical latch integrated into the two portions, (b) deformable clip/tab lock, (c) frictional/interference lock, (d) any mechanism that maintains engagement after activation  
   → **Default if confirmed: (d) any mechanism that maintains engagement after activation** — the function is described, but no narrower structure is provided

5. **What installation context should be assumed?**  
   Options: (a) ground-supported slab or flatwork reinforcement, (b) general concrete reinforcement use, (c) vertical wall reinforcement only, (d) precast-only use  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) general concrete reinforcement use** — the lower portion is mountable on a ground surface, but the broader use context should remain general unless narrowed

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Material scope | Predominantly recycled plastic |
| 2 | Rod relationship | Vertically separated, crossing, nonparallel rods |
| 3 | Portion attachment type | Any attachable connection |
| 4 | Locking mechanism type | Any mechanism maintaining engagement after activation |
| 5 | Installation context | General concrete reinforcement use |

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

The search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the core concept of a rebar chair/tie that both supports and retains intersecting or stacked reinforcement bars. The closest references are **US5893252A** and **US7461491B1**, each of which discloses a ground-supported rebar support with a lower bar-receiving structure and an upper retaining/locking structure for a crossing bar [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. No single located reference was identical in wording to the submitted disclosure, but earlier references collectively and, in some cases, individually cover the main architectural and functional elements of the invention.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Ground-supported rebar chairs with locking caps** [US5893252A; US7461491B1]
- **Intersection/crossing-bar spacers and clips** [US6910309B2; JP2022025244A; KR2021047163A]
- **One-piece chairs for nonparallel/perpendicular rebars** [US11512470B1; US11332936B1]
- **Support-plus-retainer hybrid devices in concrete reinforcement contexts** [US10570632B1; US11142919B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Summary of Coverage | Gap Status |
|---|---|---|
| F1 Two-part upper/lower body | Strongly covered by cap-on-base or connectable-body references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2] | No material gap |
| F2 Ground-supporting lower portion | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US11512470B1; US11441286B2] | No material gap |
| F3 Dual-rod cavity/separate openings | Covered in intersection-support and multi-bar holder references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US12590460B2] | No material gap |
| F4 Upper rod over and nonparallel to lower rod | Covered by intersecting/perpendicular/nonparallel bar references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US6910309B2; US11332936B1; US11512470B1] | No material gap |
| F5 Lower seat for lower rod | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US7328538B2; US11332936B1] | No material gap |
| F6 Upper saddle pressing upper rod | Partially-to-strongly covered, especially in cap/clamp/cross-bar structures [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10570632B1] | Minor terminology gap only |
| F7 Locking mechanism maintaining engagement | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2; JP2022025244A] | No material gap |
| F8 Recycled-plastic material platform | Plastic/synthetic resin broadly covered; specifically recycled plastic not clearly shown in the strongest references | Residual material-specific gap |

### Technology Trends
The prior art cluster shows longstanding development toward molded plastic rebar chairs, spacer clips, crossing-bar supports, and closure/locking mechanisms for reinforcement placement [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US7328538B2; US11512470B1]. More recent references emphasize improved installation speed, intersection retention, and multi-bar support geometry rather than wholly new support architectures [JP2022025244A; KR2021047163A; US11332936B1].

### Risk Assessment
**Novelty risk: High** on the core structural concept. The strongest earlier references disclose ground-supported rebar supports with lower support geometry, upper retention geometry, and locking closure around intersecting reinforcement bars [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. The clearest remaining distinction from the located art is the explicit **recycled-plastic** material emphasis, but that appears secondary to the main structure/function combination.

| Objective | Assess novelty by identifying earlier patent/non-patent references relevant to the disclosed rebar chair/tie apparatus |
| In-Scope | Two-part rebar tie/chair body, ground-supported lower portion, dual-bar receiving cavity, nonparallel stacked bar geometry, lower seat, upper saddle, locking mechanism, plastic/recycled-plastic material |
| Out-of-Scope | Patentability opinions, claim drafting, infringement/FTO, filing strategy, manufacturing optimization |

| Known Constraints | User requested no scoping questions beyond default assumptions; invention text was short and partly functional rather than dimensionally specific |
| Open Questions | Exact latch geometry; exact opening topology; whether “recycled plastic” is intended as limiting or merely preferred |
| Assumptions | Predominantly recycled plastic; upper rod above and nonparallel to lower rod; any attachable upper/lower connection; any activation-based locking mechanism |
| Feature Confirmation | User confirmed the 8-feature matrix including 7 core features |

The search reached strong coverage for all core features in the first round. The strongest evidence comes from older rebar-intersection support patents that already combine base support, multi-bar receiving geometry, and locking cap structures [US5893252A; US7461491B1].

## 11. Next Steps

### Immediate Recommendations
- Use the identified closest prior art set as the baseline comparison set for any further legal review: **US5893252A**, **US7461491B1**, **US6910309B2**, **US11512470B1**, and **US11332936B1**.
- If desired, a follow-on search can be limited to **material-specific differentiation** around recycled feedstock, sustainability, or manufacturing composition.

### Optional Extended Search
- Foreign-language family deepening around Japanese/Korean intersection spacers
- Broader search on recycled-polymer/recycled-resin reinforcement accessories
- Design-registration or utility-model search for rebar crossing clips/chairs

### Monitoring
- Watch newer reinforcement support filings in E04C-related subclasses involving chairs, spacers, bar clips, and intersection holders

### Prototype Validation
- Engineering testing could focus on whether any real-world performance attribute (load stability, anti-kickout geometry, install speed, or concrete flow behavior) differs from known locking-cap chair systems

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search covered patent literature robustly; non-patent literature did not materially change the picture in this session
- Some semantically retrieved references were placeholders or false positives and were removed after full-text verification
- Several semantic-path B-level references were retained on abstract-level evidence rather than full adjudication of every claim limitation
- Material-specific “recycled” limitation remains the least-covered element

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature, the core architecture appears substantially covered by earlier rebar-support prior art. A two-part body with a lower support portion and an upper locking cap/retainer is disclosed in strong earlier references [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. Ground support is also clearly taught [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US11512470B1]. Dual-bar intersection or nonparallel/crossing rebar accommodation is likewise shown in earlier references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US6910309B2; US11332936B1]. Lower-seat support is directly present, and upper retention/pressing structures are at least partially or functionally present in the closest art [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. Locking engagement between portions is clearly taught [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2].

The only feature not clearly established in the strongest references is the explicit use of **recycled** plastic rather than plastic/synthetic resin generally [US7328538B2; JP2022025244A]. That distinction appears to be a material-selection feature rather than a core structural departure from the prior-art support/tie architecture.

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The top anticipating references are **US5893252A**, **US7461491B1**, and **US6910309B2**. The first two are especially significant because they already disclose base-supported reinforcement holders with intersecting-bar receiving geometry and locking retention structures, leaving little uncovered space in the core concept.

## Limitations

- Patent-focused search; no exhaustive global utility-model search performed
- Foreign-language analysis relied on machine-translated metadata/claims where applicable
- Some semantic-path B-level references were not fully claim-mapped beyond available retrieval detail
- No legal patentability opinion provided; this is a novelty/prior-art coverage assessment only
- Search date and session scope limited to the current research round

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

The located earlier references show that the principal combination of features—ground-supported rebar chair architecture, support for one bar, retention of another crossing/nonparallel bar, and locking or cap-based closure—was already present in the prior art before the submitted invention. Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: not_novel**

The search identified multiple earlier patent references covering the core concept of a rebar chair/tie that both supports and retains intersecting or stacked reinforcement bars. The closest references are **US5893252A** and **US7461491B1**, each of which discloses a ground-supported rebar support with a lower bar-receiving structure and an upper retaining/locking structure for a crossing bar [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. No single located reference was identical in wording to the submitted disclosure, but earlier references collectively and, in some cases, individually cover the main architectural and functional elements of the invention.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Ground-supported rebar chairs with locking caps** [US5893252A; US7461491B1]
- **Intersection/crossing-bar spacers and clips** [US6910309B2; JP2022025244A; KR2021047163A]
- **One-piece chairs for nonparallel/perpendicular rebars** [US11512470B1; US11332936B1]
- **Support-plus-retainer hybrid devices in concrete reinforcement contexts** [US10570632B1; US11142919B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Summary of Coverage | Gap Status |
|---|---|---|
| F1 Two-part upper/lower body | Strongly covered by cap-on-base or connectable-body references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2] | No material gap |
| F2 Ground-supporting lower portion | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US11512470B1; US11441286B2] | No material gap |
| F3 Dual-rod cavity/separate openings | Covered in intersection-support and multi-bar holder references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US12590460B2] | No material gap |
| F4 Upper rod over and nonparallel to lower rod | Covered by intersecting/perpendicular/nonparallel bar references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US6910309B2; US11332936B1; US11512470B1] | No material gap |
| F5 Lower seat for lower rod | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US7328538B2; US11332936B1] | No material gap |
| F6 Upper saddle pressing upper rod | Partially-to-strongly covered, especially in cap/clamp/cross-bar structures [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10570632B1] | Minor terminology gap only |
| F7 Locking mechanism maintaining engagement | Strongly covered [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2; JP2022025244A] | No material gap |
| F8 Recycled-plastic material platform | Plastic/synthetic resin broadly covered; specifically recycled plastic not clearly shown in the strongest references | Residual material-specific gap |

### Technology Trends
The prior art cluster shows longstanding development toward molded plastic rebar chairs, spacer clips, crossing-bar supports, and closure/locking mechanisms for reinforcement placement [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US7328538B2; US11512470B1]. More recent references emphasize improved installation speed, intersection retention, and multi-bar support geometry rather than wholly new support architectures [JP2022025244A; KR2021047163A; US11332936B1].

### Risk Assessment
**Novelty risk: High** on the core structural concept. The strongest earlier references disclose ground-supported rebar supports with lower support geometry, upper retention geometry, and locking closure around intersecting reinforcement bars [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. The clearest remaining distinction from the located art is the explicit **recycled-plastic** material emphasis, but that appears secondary to the main structure/function combination.

## 2. Scope

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| Objective | Assess novelty by identifying earlier patent/non-patent references relevant to the disclosed rebar chair/tie apparatus |
| In-Scope | Two-part rebar tie/chair body, ground-supported lower portion, dual-bar receiving cavity, nonparallel stacked bar geometry, lower seat, upper saddle, locking mechanism, plastic/recycled-plastic material |
| Out-of-Scope | Patentability opinions, claim drafting, infringement/FTO, filing strategy, manufacturing optimization |
| Authorities | Patent literature primarily; semantic prior-art retrieval used to expand terminology |
| Languages | English plus machine-translated foreign-language patent metadata where available |
| Known Constraints | User requested no scoping questions beyond default assumptions; invention text was short and partly functional rather than dimensionally specific |
| Open Questions | Exact latch geometry; exact opening topology; whether “recycled plastic” is intended as limiting or merely preferred |
| Assumptions | Predominantly recycled plastic; upper rod above and nonparallel to lower rod; any attachable upper/lower connection; any activation-based locking mechanism |
| Feature Confirmation | User confirmed the 8-feature matrix including 7 core features |

## 3. Feature Plan (Confirmed)

| F2 | Ground-Supporting Lower Portion | Lower portion mounts on or rests on ground/support surface | base plate, feet, slab-contact base | Y | Y | Distinguishes from suspended-only clips |
| F3 | Dual-Rod Cavity With Separate Openings | Structure receives lower and upper rods through separate receiving regions | recesses, channels, openings, clip regions | Y | Y | Important for combined chair/tie function |
| F4 | Nonparallel Stacked Rod Geometry | Upper rod over and nonparallel to lower rod | perpendicular, crossing, transverse, intersecting | Y | Y | Key geometry feature |

## 11. Next Steps

### Immediate Recommendations
- Use the identified closest prior art set as the baseline comparison set for any further legal review: **US5893252A**, **US7461491B1**, **US6910309B2**, **US11512470B1**, and **US11332936B1**.
- If desired, a follow-on search can be limited to **material-specific differentiation** around recycled feedstock, sustainability, or manufacturing composition.

### Optional Extended Search
- Foreign-language family deepening around Japanese/Korean intersection spacers
- Broader search on recycled-polymer/recycled-resin reinforcement accessories
- Design-registration or utility-model search for rebar crossing clips/chairs

### Monitoring
- Watch newer reinforcement support filings in E04C-related subclasses involving chairs, spacers, bar clips, and intersection holders

### Prototype Validation
- Engineering testing could focus on whether any real-world performance attribute (load stability, anti-kickout geometry, install speed, or concrete flow behavior) differs from known locking-cap chair systems

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search covered patent literature robustly; non-patent literature did not materially change the picture in this session
- Some semantically retrieved references were placeholders or false positives and were removed after full-text verification
- Several semantic-path B-level references were retained on abstract-level evidence rather than full adjudication of every claim limitation
- Material-specific “recycled” limitation remains the least-covered element

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature, the core architecture appears substantially covered by earlier rebar-support prior art. A two-part body with a lower support portion and an upper locking cap/retainer is disclosed in strong earlier references [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. Ground support is also clearly taught [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US11512470B1]. Dual-bar intersection or nonparallel/crossing rebar accommodation is likewise shown in earlier references [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US6910309B2; US11332936B1]. Lower-seat support is directly present, and upper retention/pressing structures are at least partially or functionally present in the closest art [US5893252A; US7461491B1]. Locking engagement between portions is clearly taught [US5893252A; US7461491B1; US10808326B2].

The only feature not clearly established in the strongest references is the explicit use of **recycled** plastic rather than plastic/synthetic resin generally [US7328538B2; JP2022025244A]. That distinction appears to be a material-selection feature rather than a core structural departure from the prior-art support/tie architecture.

**Verdict: not_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: High.** The top anticipating references are **US5893252A**, **US7461491B1**, and **US6910309B2**. The first two are especially significant because they already disclose base-supported reinforcement holders with intersecting-bar receiving geometry and locking retention structures, leaving little uncovered space in the core concept.

## Limitations

- Patent-focused search; no exhaustive global utility-model search performed
- Foreign-language analysis relied on machine-translated metadata/claims where applicable
- Some semantic-path B-level references were not fully claim-mapped beyond available retrieval detail
- No legal patentability opinion provided; this is a novelty/prior-art coverage assessment only
- Search date and session scope limited to the current research round

## Verdict

**Verdict: not_novel**

The located earlier references show that the principal combination of features—ground-supported rebar chair architecture, support for one bar, retention of another crossing/nonparallel bar, and locking or cap-based closure—was already present in the prior art before the submitted invention. The recycled-plastic emphasis may remain a narrower distinguishing point, but the core device concept is already substantially represented in earlier references.'}, 'output_preview': 'Updated file **Invention Title**: Relative position of flavour-article ventilation holes for dirtiness mitigation in a Ploom aerosol-generating device  
**Domain**: Aerosol-generating / heated tobacco device mechanics  
**Key Technical Problem**: Reduce dirtiness accumulation around the insertion hole of a heating chamber after a slider-related structural change  
**Proposed Solution**: Position the flavour-generating article’s plurality of through holes within a guide portion of the heating chamber, where the chamber has a proximal opening, guide portion, and holding portion, such that airflow entry occurs in the guide portion during use  
**Key Claims**:  
- A flavour-generating article with multiple through holes from exterior to interior  
- An aerosol-generating device with a heating chamber receiving and heating the article  
- A chamber architecture including proximal opening, guide portion, and holding portion  
- The guide portion is between the opening and the holding portion and guides insertion  
- The holding portion fixes the article in position  
- When inserted and in use, the article’s through holes are located within the guide portion

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults":

1. **Is the claimed novelty focused on the spatial relationship of the article ventilation holes relative to chamber sub-portions, rather than on any special hole geometry or heating profile?**  
   Options: (a) yes, position is the main point, (b) hole geometry is also central, (c) heating control is also central  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) yes, position is the main point** — the disclosure emphasizes the relative position of the through holes within the guide portion as the operative distinction

2. **What should be assumed about the flavour-generating article format?**  
   Options: (a) rod/stick-form consumable for insertion into a chamber, (b) capsule, (c) broad article of any insertable form  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) rod/stick-form consumable for insertion into a chamber** — this is the most consistent with a longitudinal axis, insertion into a chamber, and through-hole ventilation structure

3. **What is the primary functional rationale for placing the through holes in the guide portion?**  
   Options: (a) mitigate contamination/dirtiness near the insertion opening by relocating airflow entry, (b) improve heating uniformity, (c) improve draw resistance only  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) mitigate contamination/dirtiness near the insertion opening by relocating airflow entry** — this aligns directly with the stated problem statement

4. **Should the search scope treat “guide portion” and “holding portion” broadly to include structurally equivalent chamber regions with similar functions but different names?**  
   Options: (a) yes, functional equivalents included, (b) no, exact terminology only  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) yes, functional equivalents included** — novelty searching should capture conceptually similar prior art using alternate terminology

5. **Should the search focus only on patent prior art for the chamber/article positional arrangement, excluding branding- or product-generation-specific details such as “Gen4L (Aura)” and “Ploom”?**  
   Options: (a) yes, search the technical arrangement broadly, (b) no, confine to brand/product lineage  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) yes, search the technical arrangement broadly** — brand lineage is useful context but not the core technical search target

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Main novelty focus | Position of holes relative to chamber portions |
| 2 | Article format | Rod/stick-form insertable article |
| 3 | Functional rationale | Dirtiness mitigation near insertion opening |
| 4 | Terminology scope | Include functional equivalents |
| 5 | Search scope | Broad technical prior art search |

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

The search identified multiple earlier patent references disclosing substantial portions of the confirmed core feature set for aerosol/heated-smoking devices and articles, especially: (i) aerosol articles having air-ingress regions or hole sets allowing air into the interior [EP4117466B1; US12593872B2], (ii) heating chambers/cavities with insertion openings [US11825881B2; EP4087422B1; US12599169B2], (iii) guide, loading, or upstream chamber regions between an opening and a downstream support/retention structure [EP4087422B1; US12593869B2; US12599169B2], and (iv) retention/holding structures that fix the article in a use position [US11825881B2; US12550940B2; US12593869B2]. The closest positional prior art is EP4117466B1, which places an article air ingress zone inside a device cavity and in fluid communication with a device airflow channel while the article is inserted [EP4117466B1, claim 1].

The most specific searched feature—requiring that a plurality of article through holes be positioned specifically within a guide portion located between the proximal opening and a separate holding portion during use—was not found in one earlier reference with clean, explicit disclosure of the full exact combination using the same structural segmentation. However, the core elements are heavily populated across the prior art, and several references provide partial positional overlap or adjacent structural teachings [EP4117466B1; US11825881B2; EP4087422B1; US12550940B2; US11246342B2].

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Internal article air-ingress/ventilation zone solutions**: article wrapper air-ingress zones or radial hole bands communicating with a device airflow path [EP4117466B1; US12593872B2]
- **Guide/entry/support cavity architectures**: inlet, guide rib, loading channel, support cavity, or passage-entrance geometries [EP4087422B1; US12599169B2; US12593869B2]
- **Retention/fixation structures**: ribs, retaining elements, support features, passages constricted in a retaining position [US11825881B2; US12550940B2; US12593869B2]
- **Cleanliness/maintenance-oriented chamber designs**: side openings, extractors, hydrophobic surfaces, or airflow inlets positioned to aid maintenance/cleanliness [US11246342B2; US11523635B2; EP4087422B1]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Summary of Coverage | Coverage Level | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| F1 Ventilated flavour article | Strong earlier coverage for article air ingress/holes/openings into interior [EP4117466B1; US12593872B2] | STRONG | Exact "plural through holes" appears in some refs; exact stick geometry varies |
| F2 Chamber with proximal opening | Strong earlier coverage [US11825881B2; EP4087422B1; US12599169B2] | STRONG | Well populated art area |
| F3 Guide portion before holding portion | Strong earlier coverage for guide/loading/entry region before downstream retaining/support region [EP4087422B1; US12593869B2; US12599169B2] | STRONG | Terminology varies |
| F4 Holding portion retains article | Strong earlier coverage [US11825881B2; US12550940B2; US12593869B2] | STRONG | Fixed-position retention expressly taught |
| F5 Holes positioned in guide portion in use | Partial/strong distributed coverage only; closest positional teaching in cavity/air-ingress alignment art [EP4117466B1; US11246342B2; US12593872B2] | STRONG (distributed) | Exact guide-portion placement not found verbatim in one clear earlier reference |
| F6 Dirtiness mitigation at insertion hole | Moderate earlier coverage for cleanliness/maintenance/contamination reduction [US11246342B2; US11523635B2; EP4087422B1] | MODERATE | Usually disclosed as cleanliness/maintenance rather than same exact mechanism |

### Technology / Prior-Art Trends
The search landscape shows a dense cluster in heated aerosol device cavity design, article positioning, insertion guidance, retention ribs/elements, and internal airflow management. References from Philip Morris, JT, KT&G, Altria, and others repeatedly use cavity/support/guide/retention architectures to manage insertion, airflow, and maintenance [US11825881B2; US12593869B2; EP4087422B1; US12599169B2].

### Risk Assessment
This is a prior-art coverage summary, not a patentability opinion. The aggregate novelty-search risk is **medium-to-high** because all core aspects except the exact guide-zone placement wording are strongly represented in earlier references, and the positional concept of locating article air-ingress inside an inserted cavity is already taught [EP4117466B1].

### Immediate Recommendations
- Conduct a focused continuation search centered on the exact chamber segmentation used internally for “guide portion” versus “holding portion,” especially if proprietary mechanical drawings use narrower terms than the public patent art.
- Run citation expansion around the two most structurally relevant seeds: EP4117466B1 and US11246342B2.

### Optional Extended Search
- Add JP/KR/CN family-member review for heated tobacco devices using translated terminology for entrance sleeve, throat region, holder socket, or ventilation zone.
- Review family members and prosecution equivalents for PMI, JT, KT&G, and Altria device cavity filings.

### Monitoring
- Monitor CPC/IPC areas around smoking articles, aerosol-generating devices, cavity supports, extractors, and airflow passages.

### Prototype Validation
- Compare the product embodiment against the closest prior art on three questions: (i) whether the holes are sidewall through-holes versus air-ingress zone/perforation band, (ii) whether the guide portion is structurally distinct from the holding portion, and (iii) whether the hole location is fixed specifically in the guide region during the use position.

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search quality is good for first-pass patent novelty coverage and strong for core architectural features.
- Some semantically surfaced WO numbers were false positives/noisy and were excluded from substantive reliance after full-text lookup.
- Non-English full-specification searching and image/figure-level comparison were not exhaustively performed.
- No legal patentability, inventive-step, infringement, or filing opinion is provided.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis:
- **F1** is not new in isolation based on earlier disclosures of article air-ingress zones, radial air inlet holes, or analogous ventilation structures into an article interior [EP4117466B1; US12593872B2].
- **F2** is widely disclosed in earlier heated aerosol devices having cavities/chambers/openings that receive and heat articles [US11825881B2; EP4087422B1; US12599169B2].
- **F3** is also represented in earlier loading, inlet, guide-rib, passage, and entry-channel architectures positioned upstream of a downstream use region [EP4087422B1; US12593869B2; US12599169B2].
- **F4** is strongly represented by retaining ribs, support features, and adjustable retaining elements that hold the article in a fixed or retained use position [US11825881B2; US12550940B2; US12593869B2].
- **F5** remains the narrowest differentiator. Earlier art shows article ventilation zones or airflow-relevant openings being located within the inserted device cavity and aligned with device airflow channels [EP4117466B1], and also shows air-inlet structures positioned relative to apertures/openings in operating positions [US11246342B2]. However, the exact wording that the plurality of through holes is positioned specifically within a distinct “guide portion” located between the proximal opening and the holding portion was not found as a clean explicit disclosure in one earlier reference from the validated set.
- **F6** is supported only at a functional level by cleanliness/easier-maintenance teachings [US11246342B2; US11523635B2; EP4087422B1].

On the current evidence, the search shows a crowded field with strong distributed coverage of the core architecture, but not a single validated earlier reference that fully and expressly matches the exact F1-F5 combination in the same chamber-subdivision language.

**Verdict: partially_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: Medium-High.** The strongest earlier references are EP4117466B1 for internal article air-ingress positioning in an inserted device cavity, US11825881B2 / US12550940B2 for fixed-position retention, and US12593869B2 / US12599169B2 / EP4087422B1 for guide/loading/entry structures. The main residual gap is the exact placement of plural through holes within a distinct guide portion between opening and holding portion, but adjacent teachings are substantial.

## Limitations

- Patent search only; no freedom-to-operate or infringement analysis performed.
- Primarily English-language records/abstracts and available full-text claim payloads reviewed.
- Some semantically surfaced WO references were noisy mismatches and were excluded after validation.
- No exhaustive figure-by-figure comparison or family-wide prosecution-history review was performed.
- Non-patent literature coverage was not material in this search session.

## Verdict

**Verdict: partially_novel**

Rationale: earlier references collectively disclose aerosol article ventilation structures, device cavities/heating chambers with insertion openings, guide/loading or entry geometries, and fixed-position retaining structures [EP4117466B1; US11825881B2; US12593869B2; US12599169B2; EP4087422B1]. The remaining distinction appears to be the exact spatial limitation that the plurality of article through holes sits within a specifically defined guide portion during use, rather than merely within ### Immediate Recommendations
- Conduct a focused continuation search centered on the exact chamber segmentation used internally for “guide portion” versus “holding portion,” especially if proprietary mechanical drawings use narrower terms than the public patent art.
- Run citation expansion around the two most structurally relevant seeds: EP4117466B1 and US11246342B2.

### Optional Extended Search
- Add JP/KR/CN family-member review for heated tobacco devices using translated terminology for entrance sleeve, throat region, holder socket, or ventilation zone.
- Review family members and prosecution equivalents for PMI, JT, KT&G, and Altria device cavity filings.

### Monitoring
- Monitor CPC/IPC areas around smoking articles, aerosol-generating devices, cavity supports, extractors, and airflow passages.

### Prototype Validation
- Compare the product embodiment against the closest prior art on three questions: (i) whether the holes are sidewall through-holes versus air-ingress zone/perforation band, (ii) whether the guide portion is structurally distinct from the holding portion, and (iii) whether the hole location is fixed specifically in the guide region during the use position.

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search quality is good for first-pass patent novelty coverage and strong for core architectural features.
- Some semantically surfaced WO numbers were false positives/noisy and were excluded from substantive reliance after full-text lookup.
- Non-English full-specification searching and image/figure-level comparison were not exhaustively performed.
- No legal patentability, inventive-step, infringement, or filing opinion is provided.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis:
- **F1** is not new in isolation based on earlier disclosures of article air-ingress zones, radial air inlet holes, or analogous ventilation structures into an article interior [EP4117466B1; US12593872B2].
- **F2** is widely disclosed in earlier heated aerosol devices having cavities/chambers/openings that receive and heat articles [US11825881B2; EP4087422B1; US12599169B2].
- **F3** is also represented in earlier loading, inlet, guide-rib, passage, and entry-channel architectures positioned upstream of a downstream use region [EP4087422B1; US12593869B2; US12599169B2].
- **F4** is strongly represented by retaining ribs, support features, and adjustable retaining elements that hold the article in a fixed or retained use position [US11825881B2; US12550940B2; US12593869B2].
- **F5** remains the narrowest differentiator. Earlier art shows article ventilation zones or airflow-relevant openings being located within the inserted device cavity and aligned with device airflow channels [EP4117466B1], and also shows air-inlet structures positioned relative to apertures/openings in operating positions [US11246342B2]. However, the exact wording that the plurality of through holes is positioned specifically within a distinct “guide portion” located between the proximal opening and the holding portion was not found as a clean explicit disclosure in one earlier reference from the validated set.
- **F6** is supported only at a functional level by cleanliness/easier-maintenance teachings [US11246342B2; US11523635B2; EP4087422B1].

On the current evidence, the search shows a crowded field with strong distributed coverage of the core architecture, but not a single validated earlier reference that fully and expressly matches the exact F1-F5 combination in the same chamber-subdivision language.

**Verdict: partially_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: Medium-High.** The strongest earlier references are EP4117466B1 for internal article air-ingress positioning in an inserted device cavity, US11825881B2 / US12550940B2 for fixed-position retention, and US12593869B2 / US12599169B2 / EP4087422B1 for guide/loading/entry structures. The main residual gap is the exact placement of plural through holes within a distinct guide portion between opening and holding portion, but adjacent teachings are substantial.

## Limitations

- Patent search only; no freedom-to-operate or infringement analysis performed.
- Primarily English-language records/abstracts and available full-text claim payloads reviewed.
- Some semantically surfaced WO references were noisy mismatches and were excluded after validation.
- No exhaustive figure-by-figure comparison or family-wide prosecution-history review was performed.
- Non-patent literature coverage was not material in this search session.

## Verdict

**Verdict: partially_novel**

Rationale: earlier references collectively disclose aerosol article ventilation structures, device cavities/heating chambers with insertion openings, guide/loading or entry geometries, and fixed-position retaining structures [EP4117466B1; US11825881B2; US12593869B2; US12599169B2; EP4087422B1]. The remaining distinction appears to be the exact spatial limitation that the plurality of article through holes sits within a specifically defined guide portion during use, rather than merely within **Invention Title**: Aerosol-generating article with boundary-spanning glue layer and nested tipping papers  
**Domain**: Aerosol-generating articles / heated aerosol consumables / smoking substitute articles  
**Key Technical Problem**: Improve structural integration and assembly of a multi-segment aerosol-generating article, particularly around the interface between an aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment, while maintaining wrapping integrity using tipping paper and adhesive placement.  
**Proposed Solution**: A multi-segment aerosol-generating article arranged along a central axis, including an aerosol-generating segment with a flavor source, where one or more tipping papers wrap around multiple segments, and at least one glue layer is disposed on the tipping paper at or radially outward of the boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment. In the narrower embodiment, a first tipping paper surrounds the entire aerosol-generating segment from a first end, a second tipping paper extends from a second end and surrounds the first tipping paper, and the glue layer extends at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary along the axial direction.  
**Key Claims**:  
- Multi-segment aerosol-generating article including an aerosol-generating segment with a flavor source  
- One or more tipping papers surrounding multiple segments including the aerosol-generating segment  
- Glue layer located on tipping paper at or radially outward of the boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment  
- Preferred nested tipping paper structure with first and second tipping papers  
- Preferred axial glue coverage extending at least 1 mm on both sides of the segment boundary

## Open Questions with Proposed Defaults

For each question below, **"Default if confirmed"** shows what will be assumed if you reply "Confirm defaults":

1. **Which embodiment should be treated as the main novelty target?**  
   Options: (a) the broader glue-at-boundary concept, (b) the nested first/second tipping paper embodiment, (c) both, with the broader concept primary and nested tipping papers as dependent detail  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) both, with the broader concept primary and nested tipping papers as dependent detail** — this best reflects the disclosure as written and captures both the general architecture and the narrower embodiment

2. **How should the “flavour source” be interpreted?**  
   Options: (a) broadly, as any aerosol-forming or flavor-delivering substrate/material in the aerosol-generating segment, (b) narrowly, as a distinct flavor element separate from aerosol substrate, (c) only as a tobacco-containing flavor source  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) broadly, as any aerosol-forming or flavor-delivering substrate/material in the aerosol-generating segment** — this is the broadest technically reasonable reading and most appropriate for a prior art novelty search

3. **What should be assumed about the adjacent segment?**  
   Options: (a) any segment adjacent to the aerosol-generating segment, including cooling, filter, or mouthpiece-related segments, (b) only a cooling segment, (c) only a filter segment  
   → **Default if confirmed: (a) any segment adjacent to the aerosol-generating segment, including cooling, filter, or mouthpiece-related segments** — the current disclosure does not limit the adjacent segment type

4. **How should the glue-layer positioning requirement be interpreted for searching?**  
   Options: (a) adhesive centered on or crossing the segment boundary region, (b) adhesive merely located somewhere radially outward of the boundary, (c) both interpretations, prioritizing adhesive spanning the boundary region  
   → **Default if confirmed: (c) both interpretations, prioritizing adhesive spanning the boundary region** — this captures the broad language while still targeting the more specific, technically meaningful placement

5. **Should the “at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary” limitation be treated as core or narrower fallback detail?**  
   Options: (a) core feature, (b) narrower fallback detail, (c) optional manufacturing preference only  
   → **Default if confirmed: (b) narrower fallback detail** — the 1 mm dimension appears to refine the broader glue-placement concept rather than define the whole invention

## Defaults Summary

| # | Question | Default Answer |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Main novelty target | Both; broad concept primary, nested tipping papers dependent |
| 2 | Meaning of flavour source | Broad flavor-delivering/aerosol-forming material |
| 3 | Meaning of adjacent segment | Any adjacent segment |
| 4 | Glue-layer positioning | Both interpretations; prioritize boundary-spanning adhesive |
| 5 | 1 mm both sides limitation | Narrower fallback detail |

'content': '# Invention Scope

## Customer Idea
An aerosol-generating article having a plurality of segments, including an aerosol-generating segment with a flavour source. One or more tipping papers wrap around multiple segments including the aerosol-generating segment. At least one glue layer is disposed on the tipping paper and is positioned at or radially outward of a boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment. In a narrower embodiment, a first tipping paper surrounds the entire aerosol-generating segment and a second tipping paper surrounds the first tipping paper, with the glue layer extending at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary along the central axis.

## Clarifications
1. Main novelty target: both broad glue-at-boundary concept and nested tipping paper embodiment; broad concept primary.
2. Flavour source interpreted broadly as any aerosol-forming or flavour-delivering substrate/material in the aerosol-generating segment.
3. Adjacent segment interpreted broadly as any adjacent segment including cooling, filter, or mouthpiece-related segments.
4. Glue-layer positioning interpreted broadly, with priority on adhesive spanning/crossing the boundary region.
5. 1 mm both sides limitation treated as narrower fallback detail.

## Confirmed Scope
Novelty search will focus on multi-segment aerosol-generating articles in which adhesive carried by tipping paper is deliberately positioned at a segment boundary associated with the aerosol-generating segment, especially where the adhesive spans or overlies the boundary region and where nested tipping papers are used around the aerosol-generating segment.

The search identified substantial earlier art ', 'ai_content_full': '## 1. Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

A prior-art search was conducted for a multi-segment aerosol-generating article in which tipping paper and adhesive are arranged relative to the boundary between an aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment, with a narrower embodiment involving nested first and second tipping papers and a glue region extending at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary. The search identified substantial earlier art for the general multi-segment aerosol article architecture, aerosol-generating segments with flavor or aerosol-forming material, and tipping/overwrap structures spanning multiple segments [US12514281B2; WO2007108877A2; PH22013500003U1]. It also identified earlier art for localized adhesive treatment on tipping paper in relation to segment interfaces or contact faces [US11944117B2; JP06589084B1] and earlier art for layered/overlapping wrapper arrangements [WO2014009499A2; ID201812208A].

The strongest earlier references indicate that the broad article architecture and wrapper arrangement features are well represented in the prior art. The most important remaining distinction is the exact glue-placement formulation of Feature F4 as confirmed in scope—namely a glue layer disposed on tipping paper and positioned at or radially outward of the boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment—and the narrower dimensional requirement of Feature F5. While JP06589084B1 and US11944117B2 come close, the exact boundary-centered formulation and the exact “at least 1 mm on both sides” limitation were not fully and independently established across the strongest earlier references based on the retrieved full-text payloads.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Segmented heated aerosol sticks with tipping paper or annular wrapper joining multiple axial segments** [US12514281B2; US11291243B2; WO2007108877A2]
- **Aerosol-generating articles with inner/outer or first/second wrapper concepts** [WO2014009499A2; ID201812208A; EP4640081A1]
- **Manufacturing methods using localized dense adhesive regions on chip paper/tipping paper near segment contact regions** [US11944117B2; JP06589084B1]
- **Wrapper seam/overlap adhesive engineering in aerosol articles** [RU2741415C2; US11259561B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Summary of Prior-Art Coverage | Status |
|---|---|---|
| F1 Multi-segment aerosol article | Clearly shown in multiple earlier references | Covered |
| F2 Aerosol-generating segment with flavour source | Clearly shown, though some references express this as aerosol-forming substrate rather than flavor source | Covered |
| F3 Tipping paper around multiple segments | Clearly shown in multiple earlier references | Covered |
| F4 Glue at boundary region | Partially supported to strongly suggested by adhesive-dense/contact-face references, but exact formulation remains narrower | Partially covered |
| F5 At least 1 mm on both sides of boundary | Only partial support found; exact dimensional teaching not clearly established | Weakly covered |
| F6 Nested first and second tipping papers | Partial-to-strong support from layered wrapper references, though not always in identical geometry | Partially covered |

### Technology / Assignee Trends
The search results cluster around major heated-aerosol and smoking-substitute developers, including British American Tobacco, Japan Tobacco, Philip Morris-related families, Reynolds/RJR, KT&G, and China Tobacco. Common themes include multi-segment rod construction, tipping wrapper engineering, segment abutment management, cooling/filter integration, and wrapper-based mechanical reinforcement.

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk from earlier art is **medium to high** for the broad article and wrapper architecture because several references disclose closely related segmented aerosol articles and wrapper arrangements [US12514281B2; WO2007108877A2; PH22013500003U1]. The principal unresolved issue is whether the exact boundary-positioned glue arrangement and the narrower nested wrapper implementation are fully taught in one independent earlier reference or only pieced together from multiple references. This is a prior-art coverage assessment only, not a patentability opinion.

No non-patent literature references were retained as A- or B-level references in the final screened set. ## 8. Transactional Search Summary (Client-Facing)

### Search Approach
- **Scope:** Multi-segment aerosol-generating articles with boundary-oriented glue placement on tipping paper and nested tipping-paper embodiment
- **Sources/Tools:** Patent-database searching, semantic concept search, full-text patent lookups for shortlisted references
- **Keywords/Concepts:** aerosol-generating article, tipping paper, overwrap, chip paper, boundary, interface, contact face, adhesive region, first band, second band, first wrapping sheet, second wrapping sheet
- **Time Period:** Earlier art through the search session, with emphasis on pre-2025 records and independent earlier references
- **Protocol:** Scope confirmation → feature confirmation → parallel keyword/semantic search → screening A/B/C → full-text verification of shortlisted A/B references → report synthesis

### Key Findings
- **Total unique references screened in working set:** 24
- **A-level references retained in final analysis:** 5
- **B-level references retained in final analysis:** 12
- **C-level/peripheral references noted:** 6
- **X-category references:** 0 in final independent earlier-art matrix

### Recommended Actions
- Use the retained A/B matrix as the evidence set for legal review
- Verify machine-translated JP/RU/ID records against native/full prosecution text if exact wording around glue placement becomes important
- Focus any follow-on search on exact dimensional adhesive coverage if that limitation is commercially critical

## 9. Landscape Overview (Concise)

### Classes / Themes
- Heated aerosol or smoking-substitute segmented sticks
- Tipping wrapper / annular wrapper / overwrap joining of axial segments
- Mouthpiece, cooling, filter, and substrate integration
- Adhesive engineering on wrappers, overlap regions, or interface-adjacent regions

### Notable Assignees / Applicants
- British American Tobacco
- Japan Tobacco / JT
- Philip Morris-related families
- Reynolds / RJR
- KT&G
- China Tobacco

### Density Indicators
- **High density:** segmented aerosol article architecture; wrappers spanning multiple segments
- **Moderate density:** layered or overlapping first/second wrapper structures
- **Lower density:** exact glue placement at or radially outward of segment boundary; exact ≥1 mm both-sides limitation
- **Geographic concentration:** US, WO/EP, JP, RU, CN, and related family filings

### Immediate Recommendations

### Search Quality & Limitations
- Search quality is **moderate to strong** for the broad article and wrapper architecture.
- Machine translation and abstract-only family-level semantic results create uncertainty for the narrowest features.

- No legal conclusion is provided.

## Novelty Assessment

Feature-by-feature synthesis:
- **F1:** Not novel on the search record. Independent earlier references clearly disclose multi-segment aerosol-generating or smoking articles arranged along a longitudinal axis [US12514281B2; WO2007108877A2; PH22013500003U1].
- **F2:** Largely not novel on the search record. Earlier references disclose aerosol-generating substrate, aerosol-forming material, tobacco/flavor segments, or flavor-source segments within the article [PH22013500003U1; US20190150502A1; EP4640081A1].
- **F3:** Not novel on the search record. Tipping paper, annular tipping paper, chip paper, overwrap, and equivalent wrapper structures spanning multiple segments are well represented [US12514281B2; US11291243B2; WO2007108877A2].
- **F4:** The closest earlier art is JP06589084B1 and US11944117B2, both of which describe localized adhesive treatment on chip/tipping paper in relation to the interface/contact face of adjacent members [JP06589084B1; US11944117B2]. RU2741415C2 and RU2016105248A further support wrapper-based joining/adhesive concepts [RU2741415C2; RU2016105248A]. However, the exact confirmed formulation—glue layer on tipping paper positioned at or radially outward of the boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment—was only partially established in the retained independent earlier set.
- **F5:** The exact dimensional limitation requiring the glue layer to extend at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary was not clearly established in the strongest independent earlier references. ID201812208A shows a 0.3–3 mm interface-related wrapping geometry, and US11944117B2 describes adhesive extending from the contact face, but not the exact same dimensional arrangement [ID201812208A; US11944117B2].
- **F6:** The nested first/second wrapper concept is partially supported by layered-wrapper references such as WO2014009499A2, ID201812208A, and EP4640081A1, but the exact same first-tipping-paper/second-tipping-paper geometry around the aerosol-generating segment was not conclusively established in one retained independent earlier reference [WO2014009499A2; ID201812208A; EP4640081A1].

Because the broad structural features are heavily covered while at least one core differentiating feature (F4 in its exact confirmed form, and possibly F6 depending on strict interpretation) remains only partially covered in the retained independent earlier set, the evidence supports a mixed outcome.

**Verdict: partially_novel**

## Risk Assessment

**Aggregate risk: Medium-High**

The broad concept of a segmented aerosol-generating article with wrapper-based joining is heavily represented in earlier art, and the closest retained independent references for adhesive/interface treatment are JP06589084B1 and US11944117B2, with layered-wrapper support from WO2014009499A2. The top references most relevant to the disclosed concept are JP06589084B1, US11944117B2, and WO2014009499A2 because together they cover most of the article architecture, wrapper arrangement, and boundary-adjacent adhesive concepts.

## Limitations

- Search relied primarily on accessible patent literature; no substantial NPL set was retained.
- Some foreign-language records were machine translated and may require native-language confirmation.
- Some semantic hits were family-level rather than fully verified publication-level records.
- Several close references were excluded from the main matrix under self-family/self-citation guardrails.
- Exact dimensional glue-span language (≥1 mm on both sides) remains under-covered.
- Full prosecution history and legal-status family analysis were outside scope.

## Verdict

**Verdict: partially_novel**

The search record shows strong earlier-art coverage for the general segmented aerosol article, flavor/aerosol substrate segment, and tipping-wrapper architecture. The most material residual distinctions lie in the exact formulation of glue positioned at or radially outward of the boundary and the exact nested tipping-paper/dimensional implementation. ## Customer Idea
An aerosol-generating article having a plurality of segments, including an aerosol-generating segment with a flavour source. One or more tipping papers wrap around multiple segments including the aerosol-generating segment. At least one glue layer is disposed on the tipping paper and is positioned at or radially outward of a boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment. In a narrower embodiment, a first tipping paper surrounds the entire aerosol-generating segment and a second tipping paper surrounds the first tipping paper, with the glue layer extending at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary along the central axis.

## Clarifications
1. Main novelty target: both broad glue-at-boundary concept and nested tipping paper embodiment; broad concept primary.
2. Flavour source interpreted broadly as any aerosol-forming or flavour-delivering substrate/material in the aerosol-generating segment.
3. Adjacent segment interpreted broadly as any adjacent segment including cooling, filter, or mouthpiece-related segments.
4. Glue-layer positioning interpreted broadly, with priority on adhesive spanning/crossing the boundary region.
5. 1 mm both sides limitation treated as narrower fallback detail.

Key Finding / Executive Summary

**Overall novelty indication: partially_novel**

A prior-art search was conducted for a multi-segment aerosol-generating article in which tipping paper and adhesive are arranged relative to the boundary between an aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment, with a narrower embodiment involving nested first and second tipping papers and a glue region extending at least 1 mm on both sides of the boundary. The search identified substantial earlier art for the general multi-segment aerosol article architecture, aerosol-generating segments with flavor or aerosol-forming material, and tipping/overwrap structures spanning multiple segments [US12514281B2; WO2007108877A2; PH22013500003U1]. It also identified earlier art for localized adhesive treatment on tipping paper in relation to segment interfaces or contact faces [US11944117B2; JP06589084B1] and earlier art for layered/overlapping wrapper arrangements [WO2014009499A2; ID201812208A].

The strongest earlier references indicate that the broad article architecture and wrapper arrangement features are well represented in the prior art. The most important remaining distinction is the exact glue-placement formulation of Feature F4 as confirmed in scope—namely a glue layer disposed on tipping paper and positioned at or radially outward of the boundary between the aerosol-generating segment and an adjacent segment—and the narrower dimensional requirement of Feature F5. While JP06589084B1 and US11944117B2 come close, the exact boundary-centered formulation and the exact “at least 1 mm on both sides” limitation were not fully and independently established across the strongest earlier references based on the retrieved full-text payloads.

### Types of Solutions Identified
- **Segmented heated aerosol sticks with tipping paper or annular wrapper joining multiple axial segments** [US12514281B2; US11291243B2; WO2007108877A2]
- **Aerosol-generating articles with inner/outer or first/second wrapper concepts** [WO2014009499A2; ID201812208A; EP4640081A1]
- **Manufacturing methods using localized dense adhesive regions on chip paper/tipping paper near segment contact regions** [US11944117B2; JP06589084B1]
- **Wrapper seam/overlap adhesive engineering in aerosol articles** [RU2741415C2; US11259561B2]

### Gap Analysis Table

| Feature | Summary of Prior-Art Coverage | Status |
|---|---|---|
| F1 Multi-segment aerosol article | Clearly shown in multiple earlier references | Covered |
| F2 Aerosol-generating segment with flavour source | Clearly shown, though some references express this as aerosol-forming substrate rather than flavor source | Covered |
| F3 Tipping paper around multiple segments | Clearly shown in multiple earlier references | Covered |
| F4 Glue at boundary region | Partially supported to strongly suggested by adhesive-dense/contact-face references, but exact formulation remains narrower | Partially covered |
| F5 At least 1 mm on both sides of boundary | Only partial support found; exact dimensional teaching not clearly established | Weakly covered |
| F6 Nested first and second tipping papers | Partial-to-strong support from layered wrapper references, though not always in identical geometry | Partially covered |

### Technology / Assignee Trends
The search results cluster around major heated-aerosol and smoking-substitute developers, including British American Tobacco, Japan Tobacco, Philip Morris-related families, Reynolds/RJR, KT&G, and China Tobacco. Common themes include multi-segment rod construction, tipping wrapper engineering, segment abutment management, cooling/filter integration, and wrapper-based mechanical reinforcement.

### Risk Assessment
Novelty risk from earlier art is **medium to high** for the broad article and wrapper architecture because several references disclose closely related segmented aerosol articles and wrapper arrangements [US12514281B2; WO2007108877A2; PH22013500003U1]. The principal unresolved issue is whether the exact boundary-positioned glue arrangement and the narrower nested wrapper implementation are fully taught in one independent earlier reference or only pieced together from multiple references. This is a prior-art coverage assessment only, not a patentability opinion.
